You can find him by looking for Daniel Bal in YouTube search. Those will get you editing videos. When it comes to thumbnails, tubespanner.com, they have a free thumbnail maker that you can use as part of your account. You're limited on how many you can make per month, but what you can do is you can stagger that with a free Canva account and then whatever overflow that you have, you can send it to one or the other. So you can make thumbnails in Canva and tube spanner and you can make video or edit your video content in Da Vinci Resolve and Cap Cut. Now with Da Vinci Resolve and Cap Cut, both of them have audio editing features as well. But there is also something called Audacity that is a free audio processing tool that you can use. And with that you can EQ your voice and you can do all types of like professional type treatment to your voice as well to make it sound better. that is also free. In addition to that, you let's see you're making thumbnails, you're making videos. I mean, I think that's it. When it comes to live streaming, you have OBS, which is a free live streaming tool. I think it's all you really need when it comes to free

## Story 03175
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @NickNimmin
- **Source:** yt_transcript_masterclass_nick_nimmin
- **Video ID:** 

If you're a content creator, here are three things you can start doing right now to make your videos better. First, control your lighting. When you control your lighting, it's all up to you and you can make everything look exactly how you want it to look without any outside influence. Two is to make your audio sound better. When your audio sounds good, it increases the perceived quality of your content. All you have to do is get some type of microphone closer to your face. It doesn't matter if it's something like this. It doesn't matter if it's a podcast style microphone or if it's something little and tiny like this. Just getting it closer to you is going to increase the perceived quality of your content because audio is half of the video experience. And the third thing is try to have a good time when you're communicating with the camera. If you're having a good time, people are going to feel it. If you're like, "OH MY GOD," PEOPLE ARE GOING to feel that, too. Hang in there. You got this.

Next, if you are recording indoors in a controlled environment and you have your camera there and you want to get a microphone that you're not necessarily holding, but it is just out of view so that people don't see it, but you can get great audio kind of like what I have right here. This records me and makes everything sound great, but you can't see it when you're watching my videos. We call that microphone a shotgun microphone. And you can use it that way where it's out of view, but you can also just put it right up to you and speak into it like normal as well. And you don't have to have this big foam thing on here. This just cuts down on any wind noise if you happen to be using it outside. If you take it off, you can see this is much smaller. Now, if you want to get into podcasting and you want something that will just completely isolate your voice to where even if you're recording in a somewhat loud environment, then you might want to use one of these. This is a dynamic microphone. And how this works is it picks up your voice as you're close to the microphone. But if you start moving the microphone away, then the audio is going to get quieter really fast, which makes these absolutely amazing for podcasting and recording in rooms with air conditioners on and fans on and maybe even just like a little bit of stuff going on in the house and so on. These are perfect for that. And then depending on what it is that you're doing, a microphone like this is great for doing like voiceovers and things like that. When it comes to being a content creator, the most important thing that you can do is to get out of your own way. So, what I mean by that is we tend to, because we're creatives, think that everything has to be perfect in some way. We have to make sure that everything looks a certain way. We got to make sure that everything's framed perfectly and all that. And all of those things have their place. They are important in a lot of circumstances. But these days, you can make a video on your phone sitting in a car and it can get hundreds of thousands or tens of thousands or even millions of views. So because of that, it's really important to just get out of your own way. Instead of thinking, okay, everything has to be this perfect certain way in order for me to publish this. Start thinking to yourself, okay, what value do I have and what message can I share with people? And what value can I add to others regardless of how I do it? How can I just do that at scale? How can I do it as much as humanly possible? And sometimes it might look perfect and sometimes it might sound perfect. Other times might be a little bit more dirty. It might be a little bit more kind of off. So because of that, try to just embrace the idea that everything doesn't have to be exactly perfect. It's more important to publish your videos and to get that practice in and see how people are responding to these different decisions than it is to dial in and just think that everything has to be perfect just because you think that it has to be perfect. So just get out of your own way, start making content, hang in there. You got this.

If you're a content creator, we now live in a world to where a lot of people are making off of stealing your content and uploading it to YouTube and other platforms without your permission and without you getting a cut. If you want to prevent that and give yourself a standing chance when you're actually fighting against the people that are doing that, here's what you want to make sure that you have set up on your YouTube channel. First, you want to go into the settings area down here in the bottom left hand side of the screen. Then, you want to go into upload defaults. From there, you want to make sure that your license is set to standard YouTube license. If your license is set to creative common attribution, you are literally giving people permission to copy your content. And let me show you where that's a problem. Let's say you make fishing content. Well, YouTube has a filter option where you can come over here and click on this filter. And then from this filter, you can click on creative comments. all of these fishing videos here. By having that creative common attribution has made it possible for people to steal any of these videos and then as long as they give them credit, they can publish them as their own and make money from this content. If you don't want that to happen to you, make sure that you have that setting set to standard YouTube license. And that's at least going to protect you and give you a fighting chance on YouTube as this is happening more and more at scale. Follow for more tips like this.

If you make videos for YouTube, this one thing that I'm getting ready to tell you could possibly change everything for you. Most content creators when they're making videos, they have a workflow that looks something like this. What they do is they get their video idea, they put together a script of some kind, and then they get to making the actual video. And then once they make the video itself and they upload it to YouTube while the video is uploading, then they get to the part where they make the thumbnail and then they publish the video and then they walk away. If that's your approach, you're doing it wrong and this is the better way to do it. A better workflow is to first come up with your video idea. After you have your video idea, you want to vet that idea by looking at similar channels to see if they've gotten views making content on that idea. Hop on Google Trends, see if people are actually interested in that thing at all. Hop into YouTube autocomplete and start looking for things around that type of content to see if it's something that people are even looking for on YouTube to just get a general idea of interest. Then once you confirm the interest, the next step in that process should be to come up with your thumbnail and title. And the reason that this is important and I know that this is awkward, but the reason that this is important is because when you come up with your thumbnail and title first, it ensures that when you are writing your script that you're considering, hey, what is the expectation that this thumbnail and title are creating for my viewer? And how can I meet that expectation when they click on the video and then they come in and they start experiencing my content. So, what can I say at the very beginning of my video or at the top of my script that just lets people know that they're in the right place? And how can I leverage that expectation that they might have when they click on the thumbnail or title in my script to hook that viewer? When you take that approach, you create a more seamless experience for the viewer. You are increasing your satisfaction, which is the thing that YouTube ultimately cares about because satisfied users keep coming back to YouTube and keep watching ads or they keep paying that premium membership. But most importantly for you, you create that great experience for your viewers. You give them exactly what it is that they expect when they come into the video, which results in them being like, "Hey, this is good quality content. I'm getting what I thought I was going to get here." And then they'll subscribe. They'll come back, watch more videos, and so on. This stuff isn't rocket science, but it can be challenging. So, hang in there. You got this.

This then expanded into messing with the bodies of intersex kids to try and make them either male or female, and then the bodies of all queer kids to try and make them straight. Trans people were like, "Yo, can we get medical sex change but consensually?" And doctors said, "No way." Queerness was seen as a problem of sexual development that meant they had to control our private lives. In Britain from the 1950s, they tried all kinds of stuff including electricutions and forced vomiting. The victims are still alive and were never paid compensation. Queer people rioted in the 1960s and managed to get the medical establishment off the backs of LGB people a little bit, but the NHS never really stopped doing conversion practices on intersex people and trans people. And in recent years, they've really ramped up efforts to do it on trans kids even more, especially. Conversion therapy is still legal in Britain. There are several very powerful groups trying to keep it that way, and health secretary Wes Streeting appears pretty sympathetic to them. The full story is 2 hours long and involves a live chicken. an astronaut just made this call to Mission Control to report a disturbing noise coming from Boeing ster liner uh this it's a strange noise coming through the speaker I'm put theic up next to speaker copy that at negative Butch we did not hear anything all right Butch that one came through it was kind of like a pulsing noise almost like a sonar ping I it one more time and scratch your head and see if you figure out what's going on here we go NASA has since figured out the cause the pulses were spaced exactly 1 second apart and the noise was coming from a speaker so despite being a bit of a spooky noise to hear in space it was clear from the start that it was electric interference being picked up by the speaker

## Story 03536
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** -CISqa73298

Under this bolted cover lies one of Earth's most ambitious projects. A hole barely wide enough to squeeze your head into. This is the deepest hole ever dug on Earth. The Cola super deep bore hole. A 12 km deep hole in the ground that took two decades to drill and in the process unearthed some unexpected scientific discoveries. Drilling this deep into the earth is not done often and for good reason. We simply do not have the technologies to do it quickly. But the treasures that lay below the surface have encouraged engineering startups to find a new way to drill even further. And if they succeed, it could help humans transition to renewable technologies without building new power plants. One company, Qua Energy, is looking to adapt a technology developed for nuclear fusion reactors to solve the challenge of digging holes this deep. using high-powered millimeter waves to vaporize rock instead of drilling it mechanically, potentially allowing us to drill deeper and access even hotter rocks to power geothermal power plants. They make some big claims, so I wanted to visit their facility in Houston, Texas to see the technology in action for myself. To find out what I learned, you can watch the full video, Geothermal Energy is Changing, right now on the Real Engineering channel.

---

## Story 03537
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** 3Ef6BGqgvv4

the 120 mm guns of the M1 A1 and M1 A2 are in fact German Rin metal rh1 120s manufactured under licens in the US a 5.3 M long 3.3 ton smooth Bo Cannon this large bulge in the middle of the cannon is designed to evacuate the barrel of propellant gases after each firing once the round leaves the barrel atmospheric pressure can prevent the gases from leaving and once the breach is opened the potentially harmful and explosive gas can enter the crew compartment the bore evacuator is a pretty simple solution with holes that allow gases to enter the evacuator as the round passes by this acts like a pressure Reservoir when a round leaves the barrel with this attachment the pressure is released from the forward- facing holes at the far end of the evacuator and this pushes the remaining gases outwards

## Story 03538
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** 3rRwyQaawcs

the p47 had a massive air intake just underneath the engine big enough for a man to fit in the air from this intake was not immediately funneled into the engine instead it was routed to the back of the plane first where it split into two ducks one flowed into the supercharger impeller which compressed the air and returned it to the engine carburetor while the secondary duct flowed into an intercooler to cool the air from the compressor section which had heated up as a result of the compressor ression of course this compressor section needed to be powered this was done through hot energized exhaust which was collected from the engine through this collection ring in a separate duct system this air was funneled to a lower turbine section of the supercharger before the hot exhaust was vented to Atmosphere here

---

## Story 03539
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** 3YmYnZoj4vY

In April 1943, American codereakers had intercepted a golden piece of intelligence. Admiral Yamamoto, Japan's top naval strategist and the man behind the attack on Pearl Harbor, was scheduled to fly from Rebal to an airfield near Bugenville in the Solomon Islands. The problem was Yamamoto's flight pad took him deep behind enemy lines. This was a 1,600 km trip. With its twin engines and massive fuel capacity boosted by gigantic drop tanks, the P-38 could make the 1,00 km trip out, spot and destroy the bomber transport with the admiral aboard, engage in an energyintensive battle with the A6M0 escort fighters and still have enough fuel to return to base 600 km away in Guadal Canal. No other Allied fighter could do that. 16 P-38s took part in the raid with four planes acting in the assassin's low-flying group, two in front with two in reserve behind with the remainder flying on a time delayed route in support at high altitude for the expected retaliation from nearby airfields. The pilots flew low over the ocean to avoid detection by Japanese radar and arrived 1 minute early, giving them time to jettison their drop tanks as their target broke cloud cover. One of the group's drop tanks failed to disengage right as the Japanese Admiral's A6M0 fighter support group spotted the danger and began to engage, giving the two reserve pilots their opportunity to take part. There were two transport bombers ahead, and the pilots did not know which one Yamamoto was aboard. With the danger spotted, time was ticking as the pilots of the transports began their panicked descent over the island. Energy is vital in a dog fight. speed and altitude, kinetic and potential energy. The P38s were going to be coming against the A6M0. At this altitude, the Zeros had a maneuverability advantage. A light, single engineed, extremely nippy aircraft. It was not something you wanted on your tail at slow speeds. One of the P38s reportedly received 104 hits from these zeros and tanked every single one of them. The P38 was entirely metal skinned with self-sealing fuel tanks. It was a sturdy aircraft. The A6M0, in comparison, was covered in fabric with no self-sealing fuel tanks. One hit could ruin their day if you could get them in your gun sights. American pilot reports of the day claimed down zeros, but Japanese records have zero mention of any zeros being lost. However, one thing was for sure. Both bomber transports were shot down. Amid the chaos, attribution was unclear and codereing rules were broken as one pilot claimed success over the radio, giving away that the pilots knew the admiral was aboard and thus giving away the codes were broken to anyone listening. But the mission was a success. The man responsible for Pearl Harbor was dead thanks to this plane.

---

## Story 03540
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** 4bvMkkDw5Wg

the F35 only has one engine and needs to squeeze out all the power it can get to perform a vertical Landing where the f-22's engine can generate 156 kons of thrust the f35s can generate 191 the f-35's engine has a much larger fan and bypass ducting giving it twice the bypass ratio of its F22 counterpart providing the F35 with a more efficient engine for cruise but also a higher mass flow rate for higher thrust this does come with some drawbacks air that travels around the engine core completely avoids the combustion chamber and thus misses out on the acceleration generated there this reduces the top speed of the plane the max speed of the F35 is 1.6 Mach the F22 can fly at 2.2 Mach the F35 was optimized for lighter time not speed

---

## Story 03541
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** 5nyqjg09GiM

the F14 had a huge problem and its worst feature was designed to solve it the plane Center of lift shifts backwards at supersonic speeds this would cause the nose to dip downwards and require the plane to actuate the horizontal stabilizer to counteract it which would create a huge amount of drag this is something that affects all supersonic planes but it was made even worse by the fact the f14s entire Wing actually shifted backwards as the plane gained speed to avoid the drag needed to counteract this motion the engineers of the F-14 fitted it with two extendable veins on the Leading Edge of the wing glove these were extended automatically by the flight computer as the plane accelerated the veins would extend outwards and generate more lift ahead of the plane's center of gravity counteracting the rear movement of lift on the main Wing this lift forward of the center of gravity also helped the plane to perform 7.5g turns at Mach 2 and incredible amount of aerodynamic pressure that really drives home how incredible that titanium Wing box was however in practice the veins were rarely needed while the F14 was designed to fly up to mock 2.4 the World War II scenario that would require a to never came to pass the added weight and maintenance associated with these veins were deemed unnecessary so Cruz frequently removed the actuators and welded the VIN door shut in later versions of the F14 the mechanism was simply removed in the design completely the F14 was one of the most difficult planes to maintain in the US military requiring 30 to 60 hours of maintenance for every hour flown and you can learn Why by watching the full length insane engineering of the F14 episode over on our YouTube channel real engineering

---

## Story 03542
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** 69MgN2j7Jwc

the very notion of using solid rocket boosters for a crude space shuttle was initially met with extreme concern once ignited there is little that can be done to stop them the entire colume of propellant is ignited at once there are no pumps or valves that can lower the fuel and oxidizer being delivered once ignited they stay ignited until all of the propellant is expended and this of course comes with extreme control issues that need to be engineered around to begin the engine needs to be throttled back as it expends fuel the upper section of the solid rocket booster had an 11 pointed star shape the shape then transitioned into a circular section however it wasn't constant in diameter the diameter increased towards the nozzle this design provided elevated thrust at ignition when it was needed most and then reduced gradually to lower the maximum Dynamic pressure the space shuttle experienced with the thrust decreasing by 33% 50 seconds into the flight

---

## Story 03543
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** 72yYvLLryus

buried here deep in a Japanese Mountain Pass is one of the world's rarest Technologies a 43 km long magnetic levitation train track which will eventually become part of the world's first inter city magf train connecting Tokyo and Osaka with the power of superconducting magnets these trains hover a mere 10 cm above their track while pummeling along at a mindboggling speed of 500 kmph with no physical contact with the track they can operate in any weather condition and have lower maintenance costs due to reduced friction and fewer moving Parts despite the advantages of magl trains over traditional high-speed trains the only Commercial High speeed maglev line currently in operation is just 30 km long a journey the train makes in only 8 minutes but a track this short largely defeats the advantage of high-speed maglif

---

## Story 03544
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** 7i5aK7NCp-U

This was potentially the most dangerous repair mission ever undertaken in space. This is Dr. Scott Parisinski and he's strapped by his feet trying to fix a high voltage solar panel that was struck by space debris. The first obstacle was distance. The torn array was located far from the normal reach of the Canid Arm 2. The only option was to attach an astronaut to the long boom used to inspect the bottom of the shuttle. And this carried a lot of risk. The solar panels were still live and they're a lot more wobbly than you'd imagine. And so there were concerns that there could be arcing of electricity, high voltage, high current from the solar panel into my space suit. And of course, my spacuit has a Kevlar outer, but there are metal wrist disconnects and other metal parts of my spacuit that could allow for electricity to conduct into the 100% oxygen environment of my spacuit, which would be very flammable. It'd be a very bad day for me. So, uh, engineers very graciously, you know, thought about my my safety. So, we ended up wrapping the metal parts of our space suits. >> From the images and from talking to the astronauts, the team on the ground had already figured out the problem and a potential solution. The guide wire running along the solar panels had been damaged. So, Scott needed to get out there, cut the guide wire, and reconnect them with a MacGyver repair using materials available on the ISS. And then I have here actually one of the engineered cuff links that was used on 1G mock-ups of the solar panel uh here in Houston. So this is uh actually what it looked like. This is one of the shorter ones and we had a couple of much longer ones as well that I installed. This allowed me to poke this through a hole in the solar panel on one end, one side of the damage, and then this this one ended up going on the other side, and then the load path could be uh absorbed by this piece of wire. But the cool thing, the really cool thing, and this is one of my prized possessions, this was given to me by the engineer who invented this this repair. And this is actually two pieces of cardboard from a Domino's pizza box. They were working around the clock. They worked for like 72 hours straight. But uh this engineer had this idea for these cuff links and he he cut a Domino's pizza box, tied it together with a couple pieces of string and he he threw it on the table and said, "Here, what do you think about this?" And and that became the uh the solution to save the solar panel. Dr. Perisinski helped us document the entire history of the construction of the International Space Station in an hour-ong documentary with every step of construction animated along the way.

---

## Story 03545
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** 7ogwLyAkNBU

the F14 is one of the few planes in history to ever feature a variable sweep Wing a mechanism that allows it to vary its wing angle from 20° used during takeoffs and landings to a maximum of 68° which was reached at 0.9 Mack there was even a special setting of 78° which overlapped the wings and allowed the F14 to be stowed more compactly inside the aircraft carrier this was a highly desirable ability because Wing sweep is designed igned to manage how supersonic air flow interacts with the wing this is how it works take a straight wing for a small Cessna air flow over a wing perpendicular to the freest stream air has one component the chordwise flow which is air that flows over the cord of the aeroil the cord is the imaginary line running from the Leading Edge to the trailing edge of an aeroil chordwise flow is how the wing generates lift and it does this by accelerating the air traveling over the aeroil however because the air accelerates it can hit supersonic speeds before the plane does different Wings can accelerate this flow at different rates the speed at which the flow over the wing achieves supersonic speed is called the critical mock number and generally we want to make that number as high as possible to achieve a better lift to drag ratio the variable sweep Wing allows this let's look at the airflow components over the f14s wing at its lowest sweep angle 20° here we can separate the air flow into two components the chordwise flow which is now offset by a 20° angle relative to the free stream and the second component the spanwise flow which flows along the length of the wing and does not accelerate and thus does not lower the critical mock number at lower speeds where supersonic air flow is not a worry you want as much of that air flow to be cord wise we need that lift this is especially true for a plane like the F14 it's heavy and needs to land on extremely short runways in the middle of the ocean the slower it can fly on approach the better however as the speed of the plane begins to climb we are generating more than enough lift thanks to the increased air speed and thus can afford to convert some of that air flow into spanwise flow we do this by increasing the sweep angle once at supersonic speed the primary concern is no longer critical mock number but with preventing the supersonic cone emanating from the nose of the aircraft from intersecting with the wings and lowering the lift to drag ratio So in theory adding a swing wi has a lot of benefits but it is rarely implemented due to the complexity and weight of the systems needed to make it happen and you can learn how the engineers of the air4 overcame that challenge by watching the ful length insane engineering of the F14 episode over on our YouTube channel real engineering

## Story 03547
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** 9IYRd5TnC5M

The V-22 has a bit of a public image problem when it comes to safety, but statistics show it's no more dangerous than other military helicopters, and that's thanks to the lessons learned during the V22's development. The development of the V22 Osprey began in the early 1950s with the XV3, a clunky aircraft with a piston-powered engine installed in the fuse lash behind the pilot with a two-speed transmission, allowing the rotors to operate at two speeds for hover and cruise. The rotors were mechanically linked to the engine through the wings with the entire rotor assembly rotating through gaps in the fairings. The XV3 worked fine in helicopter mode, but these linkages and pylons lack the stiffness needed to stabilize the intense vibrations and forces of transition to an entirely different mode of flight. These whirling vibrations through the skinny pylons got so intense that it knocked out test pilot Dick Stanbury, severely injuring the man and damaging the first prototype beyond repair. But Belle didn't give up on the vision. It took the lessons learned from the XV3 and pushed on. The XV15 was the next step to turn a prototype into a complete tilt rotor. Unlike the XV3, the XV15 moved the propulsion out to the fuselage and onto the wing tips. On the XV3, the drive shafts and gearboxes had to transmit all of the engine's power out to the wing tips for the entire flight. That required heavy, robust drive shafts designed for long flights at high stress. But the XV15 placed its engines on the wing tips. With the power from the engine being so much closer to the rotor, the pylons could be bolstered with a fullness that provided a lot of extra stiffness. But this created a new problem. How to synchronize the engines. The XV15 developed an engine synchronization system that would carry on through to the V22, a cross shaft through the wings to keep the rotors in sync. The only time they would need to carry a large torque would be if one engine failed, where an automatic clutch disconnects the failed engine, letting the other engine drive both rotors through the cross shaft for a single engine landing. The smaller load meant that the shafts and connections could be lighter and safer. The XV3 was the first tiltrotor aircraft to transition from vertical to horizontal flight. And to honor its achievements and contributions toward aircraft development, we've designed this t-shirt. It's available to pre-order for just another few days. So grab one now on the Nebula store with the link in the comments. And don't forget that all Nebula members get a 15% discount.

Deep within the Earth, nuclear decay generates immense quantities of heat. The Earth's core is 6,000° C, and just 0.1% of the planet's heat content could supply all of our energy needs for 2 million years. However, accessing that heat is not easy. Some countries like Iceland have it lucky with that geothermal energy reaching close to the surface. So close that geysers of boiling water erupt from the ground. Areas like this are rare, which is why geothermal energy accounts for a tiny fraction of the world's energy supply. We have a safe fision reactor under our feet, but we simply can't access it easily. The deeper you go, the hotter it gets with temperatures increasing roughly 30° per kilometer. And the hotter the temperature, the more energy we can extract, especially if we can create superc critical steam. Steam turns superc critical when it reaches a temperature of roughly 374° C and a pressure of 22 megapascals. Superc critical steam has some special characteristics that make it highly efficient at driving a steam turbine. The problem is reaching the depths where superc critical steam can be created is basically impossible in most locations with current drilling techniques because the cost of drilling increases exponentially with depth. One company is trying to change that and I visited their facility in Houston, Texas to check out their progress which you can watch by visiting the Real Engineering channel.

## Story 03555
- **Weight:** 4

- **Video ID:** bYkQn2TpF2k

If we could drill deeper holes for cheaper, we could unlock vast quantities of energy hidden below our feet. But the problem is drilling gets exponentially more expensive the deeper we go. To create a geothermal power well, you need to drill at least two bore holes. Drilling the two holes necessary for geothermal wells to a distance of 3 km would cost around $6 million. But doubling that bore hole depth to 6 km would cost $27 million. Here's why. The extreme conditions as you drill deeper makes operations extremely hostile to machinery. The heat damages drill bits and the rocks become tougher to drill through, too. So, progress begins to slow and drill bits begin to break more frequently. And replacing the drill bits gets harder the deeper you drill, too. To replace a drill bit located at the very bottom of a kilome deep bore hole with stacks of drill pipe above it requires the entire stack to be pulled up. The longer the stack, the longer it takes with drilling engineers being highly skilled and in demand professionals, their time isn't cheap. The hole also becomes more unstable as you drill deeper. Bore holes also need reinforcement with steel pipes and concrete, which also becomes more difficult and costly at deeper depths. If the bore hole does collapse, the entire drill stack could be trapped and require a new hole to be drilled. That's why geothermal energy isn't more common. It's confined to areas where geothermal heat bubbles closer to the surface where it's easier to access.

Imagine being an astronaut launching to a space station only to arrive and realize it wasn't where it was supposed to be. This actually happened to Scott Parisinski. >> We didn't have the warmest uh physical welcome to the mere space station when we arrived during STS86. There had been this collision to the outside of the spectrum module. So you could actually see even from you know quite a ways away the solar panels on the the outside of the space station had these through holes in them and and the uh they were mangled. You could see sort of a divot in one of the radiator panels. This was a a pretty beat up space station that we were approaching. On top of that the orientation of the space station really was was a jar. And as you know, our commander Jim Weatherbe was approaching and I was supporting him using the camera systems and our computer systems to help uh make angular corrections to to uh prepare us for docking. It was 6 1/2° offaxis. It was substantially off really a skew. Space station mirror had done a star alignment procedure. That's how you you kind of coordinate or calibrate your systems. They had essentially chosen the wrong star. >> To solve this problem, the shuttle crew had to fly their spacecraft in by hand. The androgynous docking system depended entirely on both vehicles being perfectly aligned and flown together with precision using laser rangefinders and an onboard calculator. The delicate course corrections began to be made. >> The final approach that we had to the Mir space station was very dynamic. You know, once you're you're on your approach, you're you're moving relatively slowly, but you know, in relative space, but you're traveling at 17,500 miles around the planet, and you've got two enormous spacecraft with jet thrusters that you don't want to inadvertently fire and potentially damage a solar panel on the outside of their space station. We were able to identify the misalignment between the two spacecraft. and uh Mike Bloomfield, the pilot on on board, input the offsets that I was able to calculate using some some grids on our our camera systems and we aligned the two docking systems exactly as they needed to be and we we came in for a for successful docking. But it it was uh you know certainly something that we had never experienced before in the Shelomir program and we were uh our hearts were racing. Let me just put it that way. the radar antenna hidden inside the nose of the F35 is the most important part of this electronic system we can see metal plates set in rows in the F35 phase array antenna the metal plates have slots cut into them and each and every one of these slots is an antenna this allows the phase array antenna to steer its radar using constructive and destructive interference if two antenna release two radar waves at the exact same time with their peaks and troughs lined up it will result in constructive interference increasing the amplitude of the radio wave if the radio waves are set 180° out of face matching the Peaks to troughs it will result in complete destructive interference canceling out the wave completely phased array antenna used this phenomenon to steer the radio waves preventing the radar from becoming a joint Beacon leading enemies straight to it

We have a plutonium problem. This is Dragonfly, NASA's flying rover designed to explore Titan, the icy moon of Saturn, 1.5 billion km away from Earth. And it will do it while being powered by the final few kg of a nuclear fuel currently left in the United States stockpile. Tucked inside this drone, nestled between thermouples, there is a small 4.8 8 kg chunk of a man-made isotope, plutonium 238. And the United States is running out of this vital material. And the shortage has already affected NASA missions like the Europa Clipper mission to Jupiter's moon, where it will need to get by with the power provided to it with its solar panels, which will receive extremely low intensity light in the distant reaches of our solar system. Many at JPL believed Europa Clipper should have been powered by plutonium 238. But with the material in short supply, it was saved for missions like Dragonfly. This isn't good. It's quite bad actually. We really need more plutonium 38 to do cool science stuff. So, what are we going to do about it? I explored this question in my video NASA's plutonium problem, which you can watch right now on the Real Engineering channel.

## Story 03568
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** gEYRIpIfnQc

The ISS has a pretty weird orbit. It orbits at a 51.6° inclination, which looks like this. It was originally supposed to fly in a 28° orbit, good for launching from Florida, but unreachable from launch sites in Kazakhstan. The orbit changed to 51.6° to bring in Russia. That number might seem arbitrary, but it was calculated down to the tenth of a degree. With this large inclination, the station passes more populated areas. The specific tenth of a degree comes because launching from Biconor at exactly 51.6°, the rocket would barely scratch the border of China. This ensured that no spent booster stages from Russian rockets would ever fall on Chinese soil. But the 51.6° inclination affects how the station is oriented with comparison to the Sun and Earth. The 51° orbit of the ISS means at certain times of year, the station stays in constant sunlight for long stretches. In those periods, the low angle of the sun causes one set of arrays to cast shadows on the others, cutting power. Even worse, parts of the same array can be in shadow while other sections and their supporting beams take the full heat of the sun. These beams that give structure to the wobbly solar panels are vulnerable. Even 20 minutes of uneven shadowing can make sections heat and expand at different rates, twisting the mass and risking damage to the entire array. To help solve this, NASA opened up a $30,000 prize to whoever could create a program that would optimize the solar panel's position while preventing problematic shadowing. This is what they came up with, a smooth turn of solar panels with slight delays to avoid these shadows. You can learn more about the problems with the solar panels on the ISS on the Real Engineering YouTube channel.

## Story 03570
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** HRolyZS8xSg

This is plutonium 238, a substance that is a single neutron indifference from plutonium 239 used in nuclear weapons. This single neutron difference changes plutonium from one of the most tightly controlled substances in the world to this. This wonderful material glows redot for hours, not for days, not for weeks or months or years, but decades. A slow consistent radiation of heat that halves every 88 years. And this heat can be used to generate electricity using the CBEC effect. The CBEC effect essentially allows us to generate an electric current through a heat differential. As charge carriers, both electrons and electron holes will move from hot to cold. So if we have two semiconductors, one with charge carriers in the form of electrons and one with charge carriers in the form of holes, the potential difference between the two semiconductors will form when a heat gradient is applied. This potential difference causes a current to flow in the external circuit. Since its discovery as a power source, plutonium 238 has been vital to NASA's missions. First used in the Apollo lunar surface experiments, it later powered the Voyager probes, which are still transmitting from interstellar space. Cassini explored Saturn with its help. The Curiosity and Perseverance rovers roamed Mars with its heat. And now Dragonfly will rely on it to explore Saturn's moon, Titan, keeping its instruments running in the extreme cold and providing the energy needed for powered flight on another planet's moon. But unfortunately, this byproduct of the nuclear enrichment process is in short supply. You can learn more about how NASA is trying to change that by watching this fulllength video on the Real Engineering

the F-117 Engineers wanted to minimize the heat signature of the plane as well as the radar signature to do this they needed to cool the exhaust and distribute it over a wider area so it could mix with ambient temperature air quicker the exhaust Outlet transitioned from a round duct at the turbine exhaust to a flat 17 to1 rectangular-shaped duct the duct was also divided horizontally into smaller ducted channels below the exhaust the fusic ended out with a slight upwards angle into what was dubbed the Platypus tail this shape spreads the heat from the exhaust over a larger area allowing it to mix with ambient air and cool down faster the extension was also covered in heat absorbing ceramic tiles that were cooled continually by bypass air coming from around the engines

## Story 03574
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** jAaw0vn7eLs

This is the internet's favorite phone, the indestructible Nokia 3310, and I put it into a Loma Field CT scanner so we can find out how this phone earned its durability meme status. But, what the CT scans don't show us is what is missing from the phone. If you look at the Nokia 3310 and its direct predecessor, the Nokia 3210, and put them in a line with their direct competition, you'll notice very quickly why they stand out. There is no antenna stub, which immediately made the phone more durable as the antenna is a major source of stress concentration during falls. Before internal antennas were practical, most cell phones used a quarter-wave monopole. Antennas evolved over time. The simplest is a pull-out wire antenna. But, as phones shrank and networks added more towers, manufacturers switched to a more compact helical antenna stub. Instead of a long vertical wire, you can coil it so it becomes a short cylinder. Helical antennas were a bit less efficient, but the denser network coverage made up for the power difference. So, where is the 3310's antenna hiding? You may not even be able to spot it in the scans without some knowledge of planar inverted F antennas. Nokia phones were the first to use them. This is it here. A planar inverted F antenna uses a metal plate. One end of the plate is connected directly to the ground through a shorting tab, and the radio feeds the plate at a point a short distance away. When transmitting, current flows from the feed across the plate and returns through the short into the ground plane, forming the resonant path that the radiates. The unusual shape of the antenna is because it is designed to resonate at two frequencies, one for each of the 2G bands. The resonant frequencies can be customized by changing the dimensions of the antenna and the slit in the middle. This simple antenna paid off. They were cheap to make, just a metal stamp. They never bent, broke, or went out of tune, adding to the durability of this phone. The Nokia 3310 is the first episode of our new Nebula exclusive series, The Anatomy of, and you can watch the episodes on the Nokia 3310 and the first-generation iPod right now, exclusively on Nebula, with the links in the comments.

## Story 03578
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** leRkyaveMLE

Designing a tank is an exercise in managing contradictions. Tanks want to install as much armor and firepower as possible, which comes with the cost of a lot of weight. To move all that, the engine needs a lot of torque, specifically low-end torque, torque that's available at low engine RPMs when the tank is just beginning to move and has the most inertia to overcome. The easiest way to do that is to increase engine displacement. Larger piston cylinders hold bigger explosions, raising the twisting force at the crankshaft. But an engine like this takes up more space within a tank hull, which means a bigger hull with more area to cover with heavy armor, which then needs a bigger engine to move around. We're just back at square one. The key to designing a world-class tank then really comes down to engine design. And tanks have used an incredible variety of engine layouts in the pursuit of low-end torque and power. The M1 Sherman of World War II was mostly packaged with whatever engines were available, and sometimes the engines available weren't powerful enough. So, this contraption was created. This is the A57 installed in the M4A4. It's really just five Chrysler inline six-cylinder engines, all driving their power to a single drive shaft, combining to create a 370-horsepower, 20.5-liter displacement, 30-cylinder multi-bank configuration. The Soviets packaged the T-64A with an innovative two-stroke opposed five-cylinder engine with two pistons in each cylinder, pushing towards each other, eliminating heavy cylinder heads and valves. While the M1A1 Abrams uses a jet engine attached to a gearbox taken from a helicopter. But the next step in tank engine development is already taking shape, and the future may well be electric. With electric motors providing instantaneous torque, they are extremely attractive. But all of that weight is going to need a lot of energy to move around. So, the next generation of tanks are going to take a leaf out of the Prius handbook. They are going hybrid. We go into way more detail about the history of tank engine development in the full-length video on our YouTube channel, Real Engineering.

This is the M1 A2 Abrams, the US Army's main battle tank, and one of the most advanced armored vehicles in the world. It's armed with a cannon over 5 m long that can punch through nearly a meter of steel from 2 km away. Depleted uranium plates protect its turret and hull. The latest upgrades added an additional anti-mine under place, reactive armor tiles over the flanks, and a trophy active protection system on top. But this raises the Abrams fully loaded combat weight to 78 tons. That's as much as a Boeing 737 on takeoff or 20 Ford F-450s stacked on top of each other. Yes, here it is racing down dirt roads at highway speeds, jumping and firing mid-air, wading through meter-deep mud, and drifting like it's a Toyota AE86. But out of the 31 Abram tanks delivered to Ukraine, 27 have been lost. And except for a single case of a direct hit from a Russian T-72 B3, every other loss has been from drone attacks, anti-tank missiles, artillery, and anti-tank mines. the p47d 4 variants and onwards were fitted with a 30-gallon tank filled with an alcohol water mixture located between the pilot and the engine in the firewall water injection increases horsepower in two ways both as a result of water's cooling effects when water is injected into the engine it absorbs heat from the air and engine in the process of evaporating this increases engine pressure and lowers the temperature of the Piston C older air is denser and thus more air fuel mixture is carried into the Piston with each stroke increasing the energy released in each Power Stroke the evaporation also cools hotpots in the engine that can cause pre-ignition of the air fuel mixture meaning the air fuel mixture ignites before the Piston is at the bottom of its compression stroke and thus is working against the power strokes in the other cylinders lowering the power output of the engine

jet engines need to compress air before it enters the combustion chamber in order to extract as much energy as possible from the fuel a normal jet engine does this with a compressor section where air enters the engine and is squeezed down into a smaller volume by the compressor blades the compressor is driven by the turbines which extract some of the energy released in the combustion chamber to drive the compression stage this type of engine has a speed limit because of all of those moving rotating Parts as the speed of the the aircraft increases the incoming air is compressed more and more as it's forced down the inlet of the engine which results in ever increasing temperatures which can eventually exceed the material limits of the compressor and turbine blades ramjets get around this by using the forward motion of the plane to ram air directly into the combustion chamber so we don't need any of the compressors or turbines at all the forward motion of the plane provides enough compression alone

we have two primary methods of levitating a train we can pull the train up using attractive forces or push the train upward using repulsive forces the pulling approach involves a combination of a magnetic iron Rail and an electromagnet that uses attractive forces to raise the body of the train this is an active system where the gap between the rail and the magnet is crucial as the strength of the attractive Force decreases with the square of the distance meaning the train needs active controls and feedback loops to ensure that these gaps stay between 8 and 12 mm if the train begins to drop the magnetic force holding it up will quickly weaken causing it to fall even further it's an unstable configuration these types of trains are referred to as electromagnetic suspension trains

## Story 03602
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** Uua_--aHuFk

The P-38's turbocharger poked out of the cowling like the muscle cars of Detroit, a hint to the power hidden inside. Two V12 turbo supercharged liquid cooled engines, the pinnacle of American engine development in 1939. The Allison V1710 itself was a compact engine with decent performance, similar to the British liquid cooled V12 Merlin engine in many respects. However, the Merlin engines integrated two-speed, two-stage superchargers. the preferred setup of the best single engine fighters of the war. The Allison V1710 came with a single stage single speed supercharger as standard installed on the back of the engine here. The P40 Warhawk was installed with just this engine and it was lousy at high alitude fighting. But with the extra space afforded to it by its iconic twin booms, the P38 could fit an additional turbocharger system behind the engine. Both superchargers and turbochargers serve the same purpose, to force air into the combustion chamber to increase compression and increase power output. To do this, we need a way to power the impeller that drives the air into the engine. The key difference between a turbocharger and a supercharger is how they are powered. Superchargers use mechanical power from the engine's drive shaft. However, turbochargers are powered by a turbine that's driven by the engine's exhaust. This reduces the direct power being taken from the engines and recovers some of the heat energy being expelled to the atmosphere. But it does come with downsides. With a mechanically powered supercharger, the throttle response is immediate. When the throttle is opened, the engine speed increases. And with a direct mechanical connection to the impeller, it spins up in unison, giving an immediate boost. However, turbochargers have lag as exhaust pressure does not increase immediately when the pilot opens the throttle. Turbochargers and superchargers both add a lot of heat to the engine inlet air which if not cooled can cause some major problems like pre-ignition of the air fuel mixture. The P38 had a very unique intercooler to deal with this problem. Most intercoolers rely on prominent air scoops to force air over a large front-facing heat exchanger. But those scoops come at a cost. They create extra drag. Johnson took a different approach. Instead of bolting on a traditional intercooler system, he built it directly into the wing's leading edge. So in the leading edge, there is a network of small tubes running the length of the wing where the turbocharged air passes through before entering the supercharger. They are cooled by air entering this small opening which exits through this hole in the wing tip. It's a strange design, but it had very little drag penalties. You can learn more about how this unique intercooler created some unintended drawbacks for the P38 by watching the fulllength video on the Real Engineering channel.

---

How does an autonomous lunar lander know when it's landed? This seems like an easy problem, but with very few sensors and communication relays available on the moon, we don't always know for sure. We can check to see if the engines have shut off and check its orientation with the inertial measurement unit. However, there is a scenario where all of these checks could indicate a successful landing. But in reality, the lander has bounced off the ground and is actually currently in freef fall back to the moon. Firefly added a surprisingly simple mechanism into its lunar lander's feet to give more data on the current whereabouts of its lander. And we got a tour of the world's first commercial lunar lander, which is currently sitting upright on the moon. >> You also see that there's no foot on that leg. Uh there's actually a hollow cylinder. This is a 3D printed titanium piece. The reason we 3D print uh not just because it's easier to manufacture. In fact, it's it's arguably not uh but because we're able to vary the infill. So, it doesn't have to be solid titanium. We can have cavities uh on the inside. Inside of that goes uh what we call a shock shuttle. It's a crushable aluminum honeycomb piece and that absorbs the impact of landing and actually compresses uh when we land. Uh so so that you know not only absorbs the shock for the payloads keeping them within the launch environment, but it also makes sure that all four feet are more likely to be in contact with the ground. If you have no compliance, right, you can you can have a foot floating now. You have an unstable platform. You know, we deploy the vacuum that that could push us over. So, we want we want compliant feet. Uh we will probably be able to show you a foot outside of the clean room when we leave here. But that also has a swiveable ankle. It's got a nice bowl shape so it doesn't catch on the lunar surface if if we came in at a at a slight angle. >> And how do they know when the lander has actually touched the ground? So we actually start with the the foot at an angle and then when we land it presumably goes flat. It goes to some other direction and it it unplugs something and that's that's all the sensor is. It's not it's not complicated and only one of them needs to trip to uh confirm landing. There is a possibility that we land at and that foot stays at the exact same angle >> but we have four feet so it's unlikely to happen on every single foot and only one of them needs to trip to uh confirm landing. We do checks on those, right? Because there is a risk that during launch one comes out. During transit, we have an intermittent connection. We never have them power off the engine until we're below a certain threshold. And we're doing checks on them throughout flight to make sure we don't have any any transient uh signals coming from that. And if we do, we say ignore that sensor during landing. Uh just so we we don't >> we don't get a false contact. >> A sensor that relies on yanking a cable out of its plug. Often the simplest design is the best one. Since 2016, China has been operating two underwater sensors. One located in the deepest point on Earth, Challenger Deep, and another off the coast of Yap, Micronia. Both within earshot of America's farthest flung military outpost in the Pacific Ocean, Guam, and directly on the path through one of the primary entry points into the South China Sea. These sensors have been tracking US naval movements for nearly a decade and that program is being expanded to cover the entire maritime border of China to ensure submarines cannot enter Chinese territory without being detected. Each cluster of acoustic sensors are estimated to have around a 160 km range and China is expanding that system to cover every entry path into the South China Sea. The system is powered through cables linked to onshore facilities delivering 10 kovts of high power direct current while each of the sensors are connected by optical fibers allowing data to be quickly shared and analyzed. Submarines rely on stealth and once they are detected they are in extreme danger. ## Story 03609
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** WW_7h-lQYio

[Music] This is the Mark50 torpedo. The Mark50 torpedo was specifically designed to attack deep diving submarines. We need a high energy density power source to achieve the speeds and range needed for a weapon like this. But there's one major problem for a device designed to dive to the extreme depths that submarines dwell in. The pressure outside the torpedo continually rises. And eventually that pressure is going to be higher than the exhaust pressure of the engine. This can be solved by actively pumping the exhaust out. But there is a more elegant solution. Storing the exhaust products on board. This torpedo is powered by a lithium fuel and a sulfur hexaflloride oxidizer. A highly unusual fuel oxidizer combination. But they have one critical property. The volume of the products of combustion is smaller than the volume of the reactants, allowing them to be stored on board without any pressure buildup. The reaction occurs inside a boiler where the heat generated is used to create steam which drives the propeller. The steam is then driven to a condenser mounted on the outside surface of the torpedo where it can be cooled by the surrounding water rushing by and then returned to the boiler as liquid water. This system also means the torpedo does not have an exhaust wake, making it harder to detect, making it a formidable foe for submarines. This is what it looks like when it finds its target.

the turbine engine of the M1 Works similarly to aircraft engines with some important differences air enters the engine here where it is compressed by the low press compressor and then the high pressure compressor each individually driven by separate turbine stages these turbine stages are driven by the combuster which works a little differently to typical aircraft combustors it is mounted perpendicularly to the engine and protrudes out of the engine there are two drive shafts in this engine a secondary drive shaft driven by the high-press turbine which runs forward to an accessory gearbox that runs things like compressors electronics and hydraulics the main drive shaft driven by a dedicated power turbine which is not connected to the compressors runs rearward to the Tank's driv sprockets this primary drive shaft and its reduction gearbox is surrounded by something called a recuperator this is the biggest difference between this turbine engine and a typical aircraft jet engine

## Story 03617
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** ZJuI5fImD3U

If space is technically freezing, why does the ISS need cooling? Everything on board the ISS generates heat. The computers, the people, and all that metal absorbs a lot of heat when in direct sunlight. To make it worse, the outside is a vacuum. The ISS is essentially an orbiting thermos flask. Without a way to radiate that heat back into space, the ISS would be an uninhabitable furnace within a day. So, the ISS engineers added these ammonia fililled cooling circuits. Ammonia is chosen for the station's external cooling loops because it stays liquid in the extreme cold of space and is much lighter than water for the same heat carrying capacity. In each truss, a pump pushes the ammonia through a closed circuit, but its toxicity means it must remain entirely outside the pressurized modules. If a leak ever occurs inside, it's safer and easier to clean water than ammonia. So the system is designed with an inside water system that carries its heat and exchanges it with the ammonia outside the pressurized sections. As ammonia heats and expands or cools and contracts, nitrogen tanks connected to the storage tanks absorb the volume change by compressing or expanding behind flexible barriers. This keeps the pressure in the loop stable in all operating conditions. For the radiators to work, they have to face the cold of space. Point them at the sun and the whole cooling system becomes useless. That's why the radiator wings need to rotate, constantly adjusting to keep their broad surfaces aimed into the void no matter how the station moves. But that creates a new challenge. How do you keep liquid ammonia flowing through plumbing that also has to spin? The answer is the thermal radiator rotary joint inside. Flexible hose couplers let ammonia flow through rotating lines. Power and data cables pass along the joint through a transfer assembly, while motors and gears control the swing itself. Altogether, the joint allows each radiator wing to sweep back and forth 210°. To learn more about the ISS and how it was built, you can watch the two-part video series on the Real Engineering YouTube channel.

## Story 03619
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealEngineering
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** ZZQQzScGryY

The P-38 solved one of the most annoying engineering challenges of World War II thanks to its unique engine configuration. It solved gun convergence. The central fuselage was like a ball turret with wings and engines attached to it. It had four 50 caliber machine guns and a 20 mm cannon, all pointing forward with zero convergence issues. Convergence issues plagued fighter planes of this era. With guns mounted directly in front of the pilot, they can easily spot their target, lead their shots according to distance and speed, and pull the trigger. However, most World War II fighters had machine guns or cannons mounted in the wings where they had more space to fit them. This raises the question, where should the guns actually point? You could point them forward and have two hit zones coming from each wing. But this spread of bullets would make aiming a nightmare and almost guarantee multiple misses in a single burst even with perfect aim. Or you can angle them in slightly so that they converged at an optimum distance in front of the pilot. But now the question was what was the optimum distance? This distance would determine how close the pilot had to be in a dog fight. For fast turning close quarter fighter on fighter action, you wanted a shortened convergence point. But as soon as the bullets passed that convergence point, they spread out at a rapid rate with shorter convergence distances spreading out even quicker. For this reason, for longer distance strafing attacks on bombers or ground targets, a longer convergence distance was better. Pilots often had their own preferred convergence distance, too, requiring ground crews to realign all their guns manually. This was a massive disadvantage that shooting forward would eliminate completely. Weapon placement was one of the more nuanced decisions engineers of World War II fighters had to make, and some radical designs were created to avoid these problems. The fearsome German BF 109 installed a massive cannon that shot directly through the engine with a little bullet tunnel that exited through the propeller hub with two smaller machine guns that shot through the propeller blades with the help of an interrupter gear. The P-38, in comparison, was armed to the gills. This level of arnament was only rivaled by the likes of the P-47 and the FW190, both of which had to deal with convergence issues. It wasn't until the introduction of the ME262 in late 1944, the first ever operational jet aircraft, that the P38 was truly outgunned. But with these troublesome early turbo jet engines, certainly not outflown. You can learn more about the P38 by watching the fulllength video on the Real Engineering channel.

## Story 03621
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @RealLifeLore
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_worldbuild_story
- **Video ID:** 0nJTKs8qKn4

After the fall of the Roman Empire, parts of southern Italy were ruled by the Greek dominated Byzantine Empire intermittently for centuries between 553 and 1130 CE. The Arabs conquered Sicily in 827 CE, which lasted for another 2 and 1/2 centuries until the Normans, another group of outsiders, conquered Sicily from them again in 1091. And then they stuck around for about another century. The Spanish crown of Aragon eventually managed to conquer Sicily by 1282 and then extended their control over the Kingdom of Naples on the Italian mainland by 1442, which ushered in centuries of direct Spanish control over the whole of southern Italy that lasted all the way up until the Treaty of Utre in 1713, after which the kingdoms of Naples and Sicily were handed over to the Austrian Hapsburgs for a couple of decades up until 1735.

The Po Valley is the largest continuous area of flat land that can be found anywhere in the whole of Southern Europe. At least if you define the geographic extent of southern Europe as the same area that the United Nations does. Southern Europe is generally rugged, hilly, and mountainous pretty much everywhere outside of the Po Valley. And this remains true within Italy with a massive Alps enclosing the valley to the north and the west and the Aenine mountains enclosing it to the south that stretches clear across the Italian peninsula all the way to the south forming the so-called spine of Italy. The Po River itself that flows through the valley is also the longest river that can be found in Italy. And it's generally regarded as the country's most navigable river for commerce and trade as well. With the lower reaches of the river extending from around modern-day Cremona out towards the Adriatic Sea being regarded as the most navigable section of river in all of Italy due to its usual year round deep and calm waters.

Because of Singapore's strategic location, directly at the most critical choke point of trade between East Asia and Europe, it was already the go-to intermediate point for traders in Asia and Europe to ship their goods to first before transferring them onto other vessels going in both directions. But following Singapore's independence from Malaysia in 1965, the government took significant steps to exacerbate this geographic advantage even further. The government of the postindependence era knew that Singapore's geographic location granted it the potential to become a major aviation hub as well with high layover demand between East Asia, Australia, India, West Asia, and Europe. And so in the late 1970s, they started painstakingly building out and designing the new Changi International Airport to have as many amenities as physically possible. That way, layover travelers flying between east and west would be attracted to layover through Singapore over anywhere else nearby.

It sits precisely at the southern tip of the Malay Peninsula with a commanding position at the entrance to the straight of Malaa, the shortest possible maritime trade route between East Asian countries like China, Japan, and Korea in the east and the oil and gas-rich Persian Gulf and the large consumer market of Europe in the west. As a result, an overwhelming amount of the oil and gas imports that flow towards China, Taiwan, Japan, and South Korea all move through the straight of Malaa right by Singapore. While an overwhelming amount of Chinese manufactured goods flow in the opposite direction through the straight of Malaa on their way to the consumer market in Europe as well. Overall, the spine of the Aenine Mountains that stretches through central and southern Italy is the most geologically active part of the peninsula and is a major fault zone which exposes southern Italy to a much greater degree of risk to earthquakes and other seismic events than northern Italy is exposed to. Over the time span of centuries, this means that southern Italy has been rocked by many more economically destructive earthquakes than northern Italy has been, which has further contributed to the region's economic disadvantages relative to the north. What that dashed line across the border between Western Sahara and Morocco that's displayed in Google Maps implies is that Western Sahara is a currently disputed territory. is the single largest active territorial dispute that's currently ongoing in the whole of Africa and it's among the largest anywhere in the world. In reality, Western Sahara is about the same size as the United Kingdom and about 80% of it is currently under the de facto control of Morocco, who refers to all of these territories as their southern provinces, including the parts they don't actually control. Meanwhile, the other 20% of Western Sahara's territory is basically an empty desert that's theoretically under the deacto control of an organization that refers to itself as the Policario Front.

In 1902, the US government began entering into negotiations with Colombia to take over the previous French-led effort at building that canal shortcut across the narrow ismas of Panama, which at the time was still a part of Colombia. But after a year of negotiations, the Americans and Colombians couldn't come to an agreement on the terms of a deal. And after the Americans grew impatient, they decided to throw their military support behind the increasingly strong Panameanian independence movement instead. The US essentially encouraged revolutionaries in Panama with promises of support to revolt against their Colombian overlords near the end of 1903. And then they deployed the US Navy on both the Atlantic and Pacific sides of the territory to prevent the Colombians from launching an amphibious assault to reassert their authority. With a virtually impassible dense jungle of the Darian Gap separating Panama from Colombia by land, the large US naval presence effectively made it impossible for the Colombians to reenter Panama and to enforce the rule of law over the territory.

From car companies like Ferrari, Lamborghini, and Fiat to high-end fashion companies like Prada and Versace. Most of the high-paying jobs in Italy in these major industries are located in the north today as a legacy of Italy's geography and history. While most of the jobs in the south are still agricultural or relate to the public sector and generally pay significantly less. The US government had always recognized the strategic and economic potential for a canal in Panama. Since before its existence, the only way for America's Navy to move ships between its east and west coasts was to sail them the extremely long way around the entirety of South America, which greatly hampered the US Navy's ability to quickly respond in force with all of its naval power at once during a crisis in either the Atlantic or the Pacific. Even a day with modern shipping technology, the Panama Canal saves about 16 days off of a ship's travel time between New York City and San Francisco when compared to going the long way around South America.

## Story 03934
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @SamONellaAcademy
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_worldbuild_story
- **Video ID:** 4vvk7i-M6V0

Hey majors, so I'd like to start off with a little biology lesson. When a species finds itself living on an island, it can start to evolve in strange ways based on the different pressures applied by the new environment. This is called island syndrome, and while it can manifest in a lot of ways, the biggest driving force is often a lack of predators. For example, the dodo lost its ability to fly, since there was nothing to flee from. The st. Killed a field mouse got twice as big since it no longer had to hide. And with no one around to bully them, the Sardinians started putting maggots in their cheese. Meat, casu, marzu, literal translation, rotten cheese. It's made by taking a perfectly good wheel of pecorino and letting a special type of fly lay eggs in it. The fly babies then work to partially digest the cheese, rendering it goopy and wet, and maybe quite tasty, and worm-filled. Now cheese is a concept is already quite suspect. It's clotted milk that you fill with bacteria and mold and let's sit for a while, but cheese is safe and delicious. Cheese is my friend. I trust cheese, so my guard would be down around casu marzu. I've learned to look past a cheese's childhood. Strange up rings are what give them their character, but it turns out those maggots are still alive. And if you don't chew well enough, they can cause enteric myiasis, which is a fancy term for fly larvae living in your intestines. Symptoms are similar to food poisoning, except with the added psychic pain of knowing that, again, your balls are full of squiggly new friends. It's for this reason that casu marzu was banned in the EU and elsewhere. A black market still exists, which is wild, and it's not a small one. In 2019, the illicit casu marzu trade was estimated to be worth 2-3 million euros annually. Personally, I would just do a prohibition style, like definitely don't put these fly eggs on this sumptuous wheel of pecorino, but if you do, you absolutely shouldn't keep it warm and damp for a week, but although it's traditional to leave the larvae alive when you eat your maggin cheese, some consumers still prefer them dead, shockingly. In that case, one puts the cheese in a sealed bag, and when the maggots run out of oxygen, they ride around and fling themselves all over the place. This is heard as a distinct pitter patter against the walls of the bag, and when the sound stops, the contents are ready to eat, like popcorn. Shark fin soup is one most of us have heard about already, mostly in reference to its effect on shark populations and the wastefulness that goes into making it. Until recently, though, I never looked into the nature of the dish itself. I figured, right, the fins are just the only part of the shark worth eating. It's probably not much different from like swordfish. Apparently, though, I had it backwards. Shark fins aren't even meat. They're made almost entirely of cartilage and collagen. They are the last part we should be eating. That's why it's only made into soup, because without being soaked in broth, it has zero flavor or nutritional value on its own. Their only redeeming quality is their unique mouth feel due to how stringly the collagen grows, and structures called serratotricia. The texture has been described as somewhere between chewy and crunchy, which I find describes most things, actually. Other adjectives present on Wikipedia include snappy, gelatinous, and sinewy. The exact sensation of eating this substance remains a mystery to me, and the unintended side effect of all this research is that I now really want to try it. Like it's a big trade. I've gotta be the one that's wrong. There is imitation shark fin soup available, but I've already decided that it's not nearly as good, so I've come up with a compromise to this controversy. Everyone on Earth gets just one bite. Say, there's 10 bites to a fin, four fins to a shark, 200 million sharks die, sure, a necessary casualty, but then we can end the practice forever. All done. You can finally rest, Mr. Ming. Come here, baby. Aww. Aki. What? Aki. Where? Aki. The Aki is a fruit originally from West Africa, which is most commonly associated with Jamaican cuisine, where it appears in such dishes as Aki and Saltfish. These alien kidneys here are called the aeroles, and they're the only part of the fruit that's actually eaten. The flavor is on the savoury side, being described as kind of nutty or bean-like. What makes the Aki controversial, though, is the effects you can cause when prepared improperly. If the aeroles are allowed to completely ripen, they're harmless, but if you eat them too early, or don't thoroughly clean off all the non-airll stuff, they can cause Jamaican vomiting sickness. This disease doesn't sound real, it sounds like it belongs next to Eastern sweats and tangyery and bone grindings, but that's actually an official term, and as for symptoms, it does what it says on the tin. Plus maybe death. Many based products aren't outright illegal in the Unit

## Story 03935
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @SamONellaAcademy
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_worldbuild_story
- **Video ID:** 5L5tjscy85U

Hey, Persons of Age. So long before the era of The Wiki, there used to be these things called Encyclopedias, where for a small fee of thousands of dollars, they would mail you a tiny fraction of the knowledge we have access to today. And you could put it on a shelf, and it would sit there and make you look learned. But while not reading it in Encyclopedia is easy, not creating one is much easier. And yet we do it. Encyclopedia Britannica is still in circulation today, and it's maintained by around 100 full-time editors and more than 4,000 contributors. So whose idea was this? Who was the first person to sit down and say, hmm, today I feel like writing down everything? To answer that, we must look nearly 2,000 years in the past, to the mid-70s AD. Pliny the Elder, author, philosopher, veteran, imperial administrator, and one curious Buccaru. Early in his life, Pliny's father took him to Rome to study law. When, as a young adult, he spent several years as a junior officer in the Roman military.
