Plants First Zombies 1 was truly video game perfection. Reviewers couldn't find even one thing wrong, with its simple addictive gameplay. Compare this to the recent Plants First Zombies 3, which people have called a game that straight-up comes from a garbage can, in which watching 30-second ads for double coin rewards is more fun than the game itself. Let's explore how Plants First Zombies eventually lost its way. From an amazing tower defense game, fallen to the extremely predatory mobile game landscape. Firstly, what made the original so damn good? At a price of just $2.99 in the App Store, with no ads or extra in-game purchases, Plants First Zombies 1 was simply amazing value. The easy first level gets players addicted immediately. It just won level the game already doctorates you into its gameplay cycle flawlessly, and in a fun way. With one 5-star review writing, I won't say that the game is a cakewalk, but it isn't particularly challenging either. Still, the game offers enough mental stimulation that I never once grew bored. This is due to the game's incredible variety, offering loads of different Plants Zombies and music, with comments stating that soundtrack alone is enough to make you want the game. This makes it so entrancing that people can play for hours, which led to crazy commercial success. In addition to almost perfect professional reviews, Plants First Zombies became the highest rated App Store product, having sold more than 300,000 copies in its first 9 days available, a new App Store launch record. Developers expanded the game to 13 other platforms, creating so much success for pop-cap games that be bought by EA for 650 million. Yep, EA. The company was such an extreme reputation for greed, there's an entire Wikipedia page dedicated to criticism of the company. People have called their use of micro transactions a plague, which sadly began to infect Plants First Zombies, because on the 15th of August 2013, Plants First Zombies 2 came out, with one key difference compared to the first. Pop-cap found a way to cram this game with as many in-app purchases and micro transactions as humanly possible. To the point where it's too much of a hindrance to me to actually enjoy the game. Plants First Zombies 2 features micro transactions. Unlike in the previous entries to the series, new Plants can be purchased with real money, as well as upgrades and coins. These items can also be bought in bundles to save some money. Certain Plants could only be accessed using cold hard cash, and while coins could be naturally earned in game, you could perhaps have $52 to instantly unlock them. The pirate season world-west levels can also be unlocked without earning all of the required stars. They cost $5.49 to instantly access. More controversial for whatever reason was the game's new art style. People argued that every single character looked to be designed by a totally different person, each of whom had their own way of drawing. There's four videos dedicated to the poorly designed Plants, with the excessive discussion about this floor being a meme within the community. This was because they'd hired a whole new team of designers, and under suspicious circumstances fired George Fenn, who designed PVZ1 all by himself. Well, an article later confirmed, EA fired Plants vs Zombies creator for refusing micro transactions in sequel, explaining that George had lost his job, as he didn't agree with the paid win model. Hit partially confirm this by tweeting, regarding recent rumors, it is true I was laid off by EA Slush PopCap, and also true that I was against making PVZ2 a freemium game. That's all I'll say on the matter for now. Short-oom is paying for a game once, and unlocking cool things by actually, you know, playing that game? You and me both, man. These elements led to videos claiming, Plants vs Zombies 1 is better than Plants vs Zombies 2, but that's not to say that the second game was awful. It featured so many different Plants, it's hard to even count them, and offered 312 levels, compared to just 50 in PVZ1. These PVZ2 levels were each set at varying levels of difficulty, better serving a wider range of players. The second game, therefore, also received amazing feedback, with critics stating the game is just as enjoyable without spending a dime, and that you'll only need a pay if you're exceptionally impatient, or just not very good at these kind of games. The microtransaction model also meant the game was totally free, leading to 25 million downloads in the first two weeks, more than PVZ1 had achieved in four years. In fact, according to Hey, I'm Jay, for every dollar the original was making, PVZ2 made 26,000, then, therefore, make the original free for every player, although this meant people would be paying with their time. ## Story 03995

- **Source:** youtube_transcript_commentary_story

Back in 2011, Angry Birds was everywhere. They had multiple different games, toys and movies, generating hundreds of millions of dollars. But by 2019, they were firing masses of staff, and since then, things have only gotten worse. Perhaps it's just karma for stealing the initial idea. Angry Birds wasn't an original concept. In April 2009, a game was released named Crush the Castle, which required players to fire a trebuchet into wooden structure hiding medieval people. Well, eight months later, in December 2009, game developer Rovio made an up-named Angry Birds, which, while clearly being a rip-off, was better in every way. It had cooler, cleaner animated graphics, and different stages each with different levels. You got points for every enemy killed, and points for every bird you didn't use, giving players the chance to compete for high scores, throwing five different birds with five different abilities, as well as a cool, catchy soundtrack, and it's easy to say that Angry Birds was better. They developed 51 games prior to Angry Birds, none of which saw even the smallest bit of success. Therefore, when Angry Birds took only two months to become the number one paid up in 60 countries, they understandably took the chance to milk it, then make loads of different varieties like Angry Birds seasons, Angry Birds space, and Angry Birds Star Wars, helping it become the number one paid up in Apple's App Store, with six to seven million paid downloads. By the end of Angry Birds first year, they'd made $10 million US dollars, but players still demanded more. We have so many fans who have asked for the plush toys, so these are going to be available in a couple of weeks, and we're really trying to build Angry Birds into this entertainment franchise. It took only four months before that announced, 60,000 Angry Birds soft toys have been sold, as the game itself became the best selling paid up of all time, within Angry Birds itself, that implement micro transactions like Mighty Eagle, which allowed players to pay to clear entire levels. With celebs like Justin Bieber even advocating for it, Angry Birds made $100 million in 2011, and went from 28 employees in the beginning of the year to 224 employees by the end of 2011. Five months later, they sent a new record. The first mobile game series to reach a billion downloads. They doubled their revenue again by the end of 2012, at which point they started to face a serious problem. The game was getting stale, they'd made multiple different Angry Birds versions, but hadn't changed the concept in over three years. As a result in the following year, their profits more than halved, with Angry Birds falling to 59th in the App Store, forcing the franchise to try a new format. They'd release Angry Birds go at the end of 2013, and while this was somewhat of a Mario Kart ripoff, people absolutely loved it. I mean, it's fun, it is good fun, it looks good, it plays well. Professional reviewers gave it 7.7, while people on Reddit called it their favorite Angry Birds game, and perfection at its finest. Rovio therefore continued to experiment, releasing an RPG game named Angry Birds Epic, which again received incredible reviews, reading, the latest Angry Birds title is a welcome addition to a franchise, which hasn't say much change, since its inception back in 2009. Similarly, user-setup might be the best RPG title I've ever played. And honestly, a pretty solid time. I think the approach they went with here really worked and paid off. The only problem was, these games didn't sell merch like the original Slingshot format, therefore merchandise sales plummeted, while totaled 2014 revenue dropped, and profits fell 73% to 10 million euros. As a result, media outlets reported, Rovio lays off 110 people as Angry Birds height fades, representing 14% of the company's workforce. They try to fix their decline with yet another experiment. Angry Birds pop, which was pretty unsuccessful. In a Reddit thread asking out of all the Angry Birds games, which one do you think is the worst? Pop was mentioned many times over. Therefore, they'd go back to basics and put out Angry Birds to the official sequel, although many called it a downgrade from the original. The sequel was filled to the brim with micro transactions, clearly annoying the fanbase who wrote, this is a very greedy game that keeps sucking money out of you. If you play well, the game becomes harder and harder until it's impossible, and then you either have to replay each level 10 times, or by gems. The game is just trying to throw you off balance, and get under your skin so that you pay more. The official IGN review said the game took significant steps backward for the massively popular series. Angry Birds 2 is missing key parts of what made previous games so universally compelling to gamers both hardcore and casual young and older. Still due to its intense monetization, Angry Birds 2 became their highest earning game. Surprisingly, however, that

Here's the truth. If you know what you're doing, making $100,000 a year becomes a reality and it's up to you to make that decision. So, whatever space that sector you want to work in, when someone who's in that space looks at your portfolio like, "Wow, we could have used this on our last campaign." That's what you want to do. It's a matching of two things. We don't want to take a big risk when we hire someone thinking, "Will they get this or won't they?" You want to close the imagination gap. It only takes three to five case studies of the work that you want to do for clients that would be joyful to give you money to help them with. And I'm going to tell you how to do this now. Give us a little brief introduction to who the client is. My client's a family-owned business for three generations. They live in Italy and they sell pasta. Number two, identify the problem. Next, you want to show process. Your ideational stuff, the stuff that you work through to arrive at an ultimate design. If you just show the design, people are a little confused as to how you work and what it's like to work with you. Very important, as you're working, to save the work and to document as you go because it's much harder to recreate this work later. One key thing here, when I look at process, I want to see a breadth of exploration. I don't want to just see little variations to one design. I want to see that you tried this thing and this thing over here and a couple things in between. And do not underestimate the value and power of mock-ups. I'm going to highly encourage you to edit out everything that doesn't fit within this criteria because when we look at work and we see something bad or not well thought out, we assume this will be the thing that you deliver to us. And so, rather than showing more, rather than showing a diversity of skills that you may or may not have, focus in on showing three to five pieces of work that demonstrate a core competency. When you master this, it'll help you get to the next stage.

So, the problem with hourly based billing or day rates is that it punishes you for being efficient. It disincentivizes you to invest in equipment, processes, plugins, and everything else that you need to make your work work faster. Now, at the end of the day, you need to know this. When you price based on hour, you create a lot of anxiety in the buyer's mind. And you've all been here before. How long will this take? What will this cost? And the answer is, I don't know. So, what we do is we remove the anxiety about the unknown by giving them a flat project fee. This is where you start to develop real skills around sales, bidding and estimating, and invoicing. So, I'm going to give you one quick tip here to seem more professional. When you're sending out an invoice, don't start the invoice at number 001. It literally is the first invoice you've ever sent. And when a client receives this, they're going to smile and say, "Oh, yeah. This person has not been in business very long." I highly recommend you start the invoice at number 00201. Even though it's your very first invoice that you've sent out, it will appear as if this is invoice number 201. This creates a little bit more confidence. And I know these are all optics, but perception is reality here.

How to go from solarpreneur to entrepreneur and double your income. First, find out what the market is paying for people like you. So, what you want to do is you want to find three people who are at or above your level and find out what their rates are and what they're charging. Then, what you do is just average the three together. So, add all three up and divide by three and that's going to be the rate. So, now I want you to act as if you could not actually do any of the work yourself and that all you're doing is managing the client relationships and the project itself. So what you do is you take the rate in which you needed to pay someone to do the work times the time in which it's going to take them to do it and you add a project management fee in. So in this case, let's just say a designer is going to work for eight days. That's $1,000 a day. That's $8,000. And to do the project management is going to be somewhere around $3,500. You add that up. But this is not where we stop because this is just to break even. The subtotal is $11,500. You need to add profit. Now you can use any number here, but I'm going to use 30%. 30% of 11,500 is an additional 3,450. This is the real profit of a company when you account for the expense of how it's going to be produced. The total of that is 14,950. Do this and you'll double your income.

Start with shorts or long form if you're a new creator? That's the money question, right? I feel like it depends on your style and the kinds of stories you want to tell. It's really easy and simple to start a shorts channel because you can tell smaller stories. You can create little series that uh may or may not do well without too much penalty. I mean, there's really no penalty with publishing anyway unless you were to actually just publish the same video over and over again, which is not okay to do. The shorts are great because you can just experiment and every day is like casting a new bait out in the ocean and just reeling in a little bit, seeing if you catch that big tuna. And sometimes you might not catch anything, sometimes you get a small fish, but if you keep going, you'll eventually catch that big fish. With my shorts channel, I did it for 60 days straight. That was the experiment. Can I go 60 days daily? And I just committed to that. And around day 30, I was only getting a couple hundred views per day on these videos. Normally, if I was counting like or if I was counting uh views, I would have given up. But I said, "No, I'm going to go 60 days." I started to get faster. Started to pay attention to what was working and what wasn't. Day 35, one of those videos hit 750,000 views.

One gorilla versus 100 men. With stats like these, I'm unsure why this was ever even a debate. I think what people are getting caught up on is the gorilla's power stat. Obviously, it dwarfs the human's power stat by a wide margin. The gorilla's arms are roughly six times stronger than a human's, so every swing of their fists has the potential for devastating impact, possibly resulting in the paralysis or concussed status effect. They also have one of the highest bite forces in the game, outclassing even the hyena, a build which literally relies on its bite force in order to survive on diet of bone marrow. Realistically, no human is tanking his attacks well. But that's the other main aspect I think people are missing. Tanking attacks. Humans have terrible defenses with no shell, no blubber, no scale armor, no nothing. However, the same is true of the gorilla. Gorillas have more fur than humans, but other than that, they're just as vulnerable to all damage types. and on occasion get one-shotted by leopards. In a battle against a hundred other players, it's more of a tank test than anything else. Unless you have an AoE attack that can deal damage to multiple enemies at once, it's pretty much inevitable that you're going to take a lot of hits. Gorilla mains are under no delusions about the reliability of their defenses and tend to rely more on intimidation than actual brawling to secure territory. And when their intimidation attempts fail, they often flee from battle even against lesser foes. So, no. Even against unarmed humans, I still think the gorilla is getting beaten down fairly quickly. The human's power stat might be lower than the gorillas, but it's still far from zero. Lots of comments have pointed out that the humans could try to score a critical hit. ## Story 04321
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @TierZoo
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_worldbuild_story
- **Video ID:** d-ubcgz1xjE

Which insect truly rules the air? A lot of you might immediately jump to the dragonfly, and for good reason. They have the highest hunting success rate of any predator, catching their prey 95% of the time. They owe this to their incredibly high mobility and perception stats. Their compound eyes are such an effective targeting system that once they lock on, no amount of evasive maneuvers can shake the dragonfly's focus. And their specialized, independently moving pairs of wings offer such a massive speed and agility bonus that dragonflies have full 360° mobility, can easily dodge attacks, and can even utilize acrobatic maneuvers just to cool themselves down after a long flight. So, it's no surprise players rank dragonflies so highly. But, if we're being honest, dragonflies aren't exactly fighters. Its actual combat stats are quite low, so most dragonfly mains focus on capturing fast, but ultimately helpless opponents. Okay, well, if not the dragonfly, then what? Well, how about wasps? Wasps are vicious predators armed with powerful venomous stingers and sharp jaws. Of all the insects, wasps may be the most experienced combatants, as they regularly hunt spiders many times their own size and take them down solo with a single paralyzing sting. However, wasps are nowhere near as maneuverable in the air and typically only engage in combat on foot. They're certainly powerful, but in terms of air superiority, there's no way they're keeping pace with the more nimble flyers. But, there is an insect which both the dragonfly and wasp fear. An insect with both paralyzing venom and the agility required to utilize it in aerial combat, the robber fly. Robber flies may be the most underrated insect in the game, with stats approaching best-in-class for mobility, power, and perception. As part of the true fly faction, robber flies do not have four wings like a dragonfly or wasp. Instead, they only have two, replacing the back pair with halteres, essentially gyroscopes that buff the robber fly's stability and spatial awareness. So, even with only two wings, they can still hover and move in acrobatic ways all the same. Their huge compound eyes grant them full 360° vision and can track prey with guided missile level precision. And rather than a stinger, robber flies sport a venomous beak, which they use to paralyze and liquefy their prey. Robber flies ambush their prey mid-flight, tackle it out of the air, then wrestle it into submission before eventually paralyzing with their venomous beak. In rare cases, robber flies have even been observed attacking birds. So, when it comes to the flying insect tier list, there's a good reason every other insect fears the robber fly.

## Story 04322
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @TierZoo
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_worldbuild_story
- **Video ID:** fLcnBxLY8-g

The Frog Tier list rapid fire edition. In general, the frog's strengths are its high burst mobility because of its jump, its camouflage, its swimming ability, and its ability to passively regenerate stamina in the water by breathing through its skin. The frog's core weaknesses are terrible defensive stats, a lack of powerful offensive moves, and an unbelievably bad early game. So, which frog builds are best optimized to take advantage of the frog's strengths and mitigate its weaknesses? F tier tree frogs speced into cling pads rather than webbed feet. Nerfing its ability to swim but buffing its climbing ability turns the incredible jumping ability of the frog into more of a liability than an advantage. Rather than being a powerful escape option in a pinch, jumping from branch to branch is a massive risk that can easily result in the frog falling to its doom. What good is dodging an oncoming attack if it means taking heavy damage regardless? Being able to grief insect means at porch lights is not worth it for that. Dtier. Unfortunately, almost every other frog, leopard frogs, wood frogs, pickle frogs, etc. Yes, their jumping and swimming abilities are solid, but man, those combat stats are abysmal. They get bodied by essentially everything, oozing to birds, turtles, fish, snakes, aquatic mammals, and even some insects. See here, the common toad, a tankier, more defensive variant of the frog, and one that's also less reliant on water. Their higher defense and HP stats allow them to push the limits of what frogs can successfully take down, so they don't fold to counterattacks quite as easily. Toads are also poisonous, though in most cases this poison is pretty mild. Btier poison dart frog has the lowest stats of any frog build, but in exchange has some of the most potent toxins in the game, which it uses as a defense against predators. Poison dart frogs are way overinvested in the poison skill tree and contain enough toxin to wipe out an entire pack of wolves. Even though realistically the largest predators that would ever target a tiny frog like this would probably be something like a small bird or snake. Probably could have saved some of those evolution points for buffing their other stats. But hey, as a defense, it still is extremely effective. So even though the rest of their kit is pretty lackluster, they do deserve a high mid-tier placement. A tier, the bullfrog. Most pawn frogs are low tier at best, but bullfrogs undoubtedly buck this trend. Their ability kit is essentially identical. The only difference is much higher base stats, enabling them to turn the tide on a lot of traditionally bad matchups. One of the few builds in the game that has become invasive from North America into Europe and Asia rather than the other way around. And speaking of invasives, S tier, the cane toad. Similar stats to that of a bullfrog, but with the defensive stats of a toad and toxins potent enough to thwart most attackers, though nowhere near as poisonous as the dart frog. So overpowered that they completely dominate Australian servers.

the hummingbird may be one of the most difficult characters to play in the entire game hummingbirds have paper thin defenses and a Max HP stat so low that basically anything One-Shots them however they have by far the highest agility of any bird so if you really know what you're doing you can command a huge territory while simultaneously being nearly Untouchable to Predator attacks so for players looking to main hummingbird here are some tips the hummingbird's omnidirectional flight ability has quite literally the highest stamina cost in the entire game and so in order to maintain sufficient energy levels hummingbird's must find an incredible amount of nectar every single day the best way to do this consistently is to plan out the exact route ahead of time and hit all locations as quickly as possible number two figuring out where the best nectar hotspots are is only half the battle other players will be seeking out these pois as well and if you can't defend them you might end up with nothing luckily whether it's butterflies bees or even other hummingbirds your beak and wings can be used to deal moderate damage and more importantly to shove other players out of the way so that you can access the best loot remember when it comes to pollinator gameplay pretty much every player is on a knife's edge as far as finding food and even a few missed opportunities can mean game over so while your combat abilities are fairly limited winning the race of the nectar and denying arrival the energy recharge they needed is as good as or better than inflicting direct damage to them so don't get caught in full-on scraps or duels but do Bully your way to the nectar by any means necessary number two and A2 okay so this sort of plays into both points one and two but as the meta progresses and the human character becomes more dominant and over centralizing it's worth knowing how to handle yourself around their bases too in addition to cultivating flowers themselves some human players even deliberately construct nectar dispensers just because they can these pois are literally to die for and should be fiercely fought for and defended to Last Breath a single hummingbird feeder can supply a full day's worth of nectar by itself meaning a single one is worth around a th000 flowers if you can lock it down a word of caution though because the reward is so great these pois are hotly contested not just by other hummingbirds but by Predator players looking to take advantage of cre pollinators look out for large spiders Snak Mak lizards and mantises posting up on or near these feeders as they could easily One-Shot you if you get careless but perhaps the biggest threat is the house cat the house cat is one of the few characters in the game with the reaction speed and accuracy to actually hit a moving hummingbird number three torpor once the sun sets and the temperature drops you need to find a spot to crash in order to conserve enough stamina to be able to get up the next day you'll need to completely shut down you'll activate a special ability called tpor afflicting you with the unconscious status effect for around 8 hours but preserving your remaining energy Reserve this obviously puts you into an incredibly vulnerable position as you'll no longer be able to use your unmatched agility to dodge incoming attacks it's for this reason you'll need to make sure that the spot you choose is as safe as possible one Saving Grace the hummingbird has in this regard is their lightness you can perch on a branch that is too thin to support the weight of any large Predator who attempts to climb up and attack you however this does leave you potentially open to an attack from another bird player so try to find a branch that is at least somewhat out of sight from above as well even with the best tips you're still going to need all the luck you can get yeah

The lizard tier list rapid fire edition. D tier the skink. Subpar stats across the board. Relies heavily on the automy or tail drop ability to escape combat, but this ability has an absurdly long cooldown and cuts your max HP. Still, a skilled player can make this build work. Yoink. Ctier. The gecko has a unique perk that basically allows you to climb anything, even smooth glass. This can be great both for escaping danger and accessing food sources that would otherwise be hard to reach. However, geckos have the lowest defense stat of any lizard as they do not have scale armor. So, versatile as they are, if they aren't careful, they can easily get one shot. Next in C tier, the Hila monster. One of the tankier lizard builds with one of the highest damage outputs in the game for its size. It owes this high damage not to its actual base power, but to its venomous bite, a rare ability among lizards. Terrible mobility stat though, which can be an issue if it ever needs to chase down a target or flee from battle. Dier, the iguana. Its tail whip attack allows it to control a solid defensive zone around itself in all directions, enabling it to defend itself from group attacks. Tail whip's damage is on the low side, though, so a persistent attacker could potentially just eat the damage in order to break through and land its own attack. Has the ability to drop its tail, but never really wants to have to use it because it removes its best combat tool in the process. Also B tier, the chameleon has a longrange tether grab, extremely effective for sniping insects. Has the color change perk, which can be useful for both camouflage and intimidation. Not quite powerful enough to merit high tier status, though. A tier, the Teu. Similar to the iguana, but trades a bit of mobility for greater bulk and defense. Able to tank a lot of damage, making them very difficult to defend eggs or nests from. Great base stats, intelligent, and omnivorous. Nothing outright broken, but with no major weaknesses, Teus have been very successful at invading new servers. S tier, monitor lizards. They have everything. They've got the best stats. A combination of great mobility, power, size, and intelligence. Similar to the iguana, monitor lizards have an extremely powerful tail whip capable of deterring even top tier predators. Monitor lizards also have a venomous bite, which again is a rarity in the lizard faction. The largest monitor, the Komodo Dragon, is powerful enough to bring down basically

This isn't the standard YouTuber burnout apology video and it's not, oh, why is me, I'm leaving forever. I'm going somewhere with this, literally. I've been throwing stuff at the internet since 1999 and for many, many years, that stuff went almost nowhere. I had occasional bits of success but I could never make any of them last long term. I remember thinking so many times during all those years, will any of this stuff I'm making ever work? Well, this did. I didn't know that when I was filming the first video. Those for the series that was then called Things You Might Not Know. I just held out my phone at arm's length and talked into it for 90 seconds with almost no research. I really don't like those videos now. But the first of them was published exactly 10 years before this one, to the minute, 4 p.m. January the first, 2014. For the first month of that format, I was publishing a video almost every day and then I settled down. One video a week, mostly on location, near windswept infrastructure, but there's computer science and linguistics in there to an occasional green screen animated videos. I experimented with other formats on other days but the rule I set myself was, Monday 4 p.m. something interesting. I never got to space. I never got to the ocean depths and I never got to fly harnessed underneath the helicopter. I couldn't find excuse to do that one, but I never missed a week. There's been a few guest videos, of course, and occasionally some blatant filler. One time I just uploaded two and a half hours of unedited footage of garlic bread flying to the edge of space and that turned into one of the most viewed videos I've ever had. Sometimes a video would be a day or two early or late for one reason or another, but to my own satisfaction, which is ultimately the only thing I'm counting, there's been a video a week for 10 years. I never broke the streak. I don't know when I decided to try for 10 years. It felt like a good round number to reach as the YouTube game changed over the years as the channel became bigger. This is how much Minecraft YouTubers actually make. Let's start with what I like to call the gold tier Minecraft YouTubers. These are creators with 0 to 100,000 subscribers. They post mostly for fun, and they rely almost entirely on ad revenue. And that ad revenue usually pays about $4.55 for every 1,000 views. So depending on how often they upload and how many views they get, they're likely making up to $40,000 a year. But things change fast when you reach the iron tier of Minecraft YouTubers. These are creators with 100,000 to million subscribers. And this is where the income jumps because now they're landing integrated and even fully dedicated brand deals, some of which pay CPMs of up to $40 per 1,000 views, which means they could be earning up to $150,000 a year. But the final group makes the biggest leap of all. The diamond tier Minecraft YouTubers with 1 million subscribers and above. They don't just earn from ads and sponsors. They're pulling in merch sales, highle partnerships, and deals most people never even hear about. And that's why they can make up to $2 million a year. Sounds like Minecraft is my new retirement

Still three public uploads in 90 days, but now you need either 4,000 watch hours in the past year or 10 million shorts views in the last 90 days. Here's how to stop YouTube from saving your watch history. First, open the YouTube app. Then, tap your profile picture and tap settings. That's where all your privacy controls live. From there, hit manage all history. This takes you outside the regular app straight into your account's activity settings. Once you're in, tap the tab that says controls. Now, look for the option that says include the YouTube videos you watch. This box is what tracks every video you've ever watched. Even the ones you don't want to admit to. Uncheck that box and just like that, you're done.

Here's the fastest way to hit 4,000 watch hours on YouTube. First, stop focusing on shorts. Short watch time doesn't count toward your 4,000 hours at all. And worse, chasing shorts pulls your attention away from the content that actually moves the number. What you want to make is long- form videos. But here's the thing, don't just make them long for the sake of it. Because what matters is that people actually stay and keep watching. A 20-minute video people finish beats a 45minute video people click off of after 2 minutes every single time. So make content people want to finish and then inside that video add end screen cards and playlists that push viewers into your next video. And if you really want to push it further, start going live. Live streams count towards your 4,000 hours in real time. And a single 2-hour live stream with even a small audience can do what takes a dozen regular videos to achieve.

If your channel's demonetized, here's what to do. First, check why the monetization was [music] turned off. Before you panic, YouTube usually tells you exactly what triggered it. One of the most common reasons is using content you don't own. This includes music or videos that you didn't make yourself. If it's not yours, you need permission [music] or it needs to clearly follow fair use. If it doesn't, ads can and will be removed fast. Next, look at your content style. If most of your videos are not advertiser friendly, YouTube can stop ads on the entire channel. It's not about one video, it's about patterns. That includes hateful, violent, or extremely aggressive content. The fix is simple, but not easy. Just clean up older videos by editing them to be more advertiser friendly, or by unlisting or removing them altogether. Finally, check your AdSense account. Sometimes monetization isn't removed, [music] it's just paused or hasn't been even started. If it's paused, activate it. And if it hasn't been started, then just link an active AdSense [music] account. Fix the issues you two flagged, then apply again if needed.

If you want your videos to go viral, stop using YouTube tags. Because YouTube now scans every frame of your video. It reads the text on your thumbnail, understands what the image is showing, and transcribes every word you say. It then takes all of that information and uses it to find the right audience for your video automatically. But, you still need people to click, which is why all of that focus you used to put into keywords and tags should now go into making a great title. Think about who's searching your topic and what would make them choose your video over every other one on the page. Keep your keyword in the title, but go further. If the other video says, "How to file your taxes?" and yours says, "How to file taxes in 5 minutes?" you're telling the viewer something different, and that gets more clicks, which moves you up in the algorithm.

If you're about to post a video to YouTube, this is the best time to post. Starting with the day, a recent study analyzed over 1 million YouTube videos to find out exactly when views peak. The study found that Saturday and Sunday are the worst days to post, followed by Monday and Tuesday, and then Thursday and Friday, which means the best day to post, it's actually Wednesday. But, what about the best time to post? Well, that's where things get a little bit tricky. Because over 40% of YouTube users are working age adults, meaning they don't watch in the morning, they watch after work, between 3:00 and 5:00 p.m., which is why the study found the best time to post was actually 4:00 p.m. But, there's one more thing you need to consider when posting your videos, your audience. Open YouTube Studio, click analytics, and then go to the audience tab. Scroll down and you'll see a heat map showing when your viewers are actually online. The darker the purple, the better the time to post. So, start posting Wednesdays at 4:00 p.m. Then, track your audience data and adjust from there.

much YouTube shorts paid us for 6 million views. Starting with what we would have made if those 6 million views were long-form views. With a $5 CPM, 6 million views would bring in about $30,000. And with an RPM of $2.60, our real payout would be about $15,600. But shorts pay nowhere near that. Short ads do not play on your videos. They play between videos in the feed. [music] So, all the money from those ads goes into one big shared pot for every creator. And if you use copyrighted music, the record labels can take half of your share before you see anything. Then YouTube still takes its usual 55% cut after that. That's why shorts payouts is low compared to long-form. So, how much did YouTube pay us for 6 million shorts views? Well, YouTube doesn't give you a hard CPM for shorts like they do for long-form. They only give you an RPM. And ours was about 13.8 cents. At that rate, 6 million views comes out to a whopping $832.

That's why a lot of creators don't even bother with tags anymore. But, here's the thing. Tags still give you a small boost. And a small boost might be exactly what pushes your video in front of the right viewer. Start broad. If your video is about monetization, add tags like making money online and YouTube monetization. Then get specific. 4,000 watch hours, how to monetize your YouTube channel. Broad tags bring in viewers who are interested in the general topic. Specific tags catch the people searching for exactly what you made. A few well-chosen tags. That's all you need. Subscribe for more tips on growing your channel.

This is how many subscribers we got from 6 million shorts views. The average conversion rate for long- form videos is around 1 to 2%. Which is about 20 subscribers per 1,000 views. While the average conversion rate for shorts is also around 1 to as much as 3%. But it's not that simple. What? Because one study found that conversion changes based on how many subscribers you already have. The study was created and led by the godfather of YouTube, Patty Galloway. At first, the study found that long- form videos convert more subscribers per 10,000 views than shorts. But for channels with over 1 million subscribers, the opposite happened. Their shorts actually converted more subscribers than their long- form videos. So, the best method for getting more subscribers depends not just on how good your shorts are, but also the size of your channel. So, how many subscribers did we get from 6 million shorts views? - **Video ID:** 

Here's how to reach 4,000 hours of watch time on YouTube. First, to apply for YouTube monetization through the YouTube Partner Program, you need two things. You'll need at least 1,000 subscribers and 4,000 valid public watch hours in the last 12 months. That watch time is what proves people are actually watching your content, not just clicking and leaving. But you don't need viral videos, you just need math. 4,000 hours equals 240,000 minutes of watch time. And the easiest way to estimate how many views you need is by looking at your average view duration. To find that, open YouTube Studio, click analytics, [music] then go to content, and look for average view duration. That number tells you how many minutes the average viewer watches per view. Once you have that, the rest is simple. If each view gives you about 2 minutes watched, you'll need around 120,000 views total. If your average view is 5 minutes, you only need 48,000 views. At 10 minutes per view, that drops to 24,000 views. And if you're averaging 20 minutes per watch video, you only need 12,000 views to hit monetization.

Why do you 90% of shorts die in the first 3 seconds? There's a metric YouTube uses called VVSA, views versus swiped away. It measures how many people actually watch your shorts versus how many swipe right past it. If you're under 60%, people weren't grabbed [music] by the first thing you said or the first thing they saw, so they swiped away. Here's what most creators miss. [music] Your first frame is your thumbnail. On long form, people click a thumbnail and then watch. On shorts, [music] they're already watching. So, the very first image that appears on screen, before you even say a word, that's what decides if someone stays [music] or swipes. Most creators don't think about it. They start with a blank wall or their face mid-blink or [music] nothing happening at all. So, what works? Movement, action, something unexpected. Maybe you're mid-sentence. Maybe you're holding something or doing something interesting. Maybe there's simply an attention-grabbing object in the frame. Don't ease into it. Start in the middle of the action. Check your VVSA in YouTube Studio. If it's under 60%, your first frame needs work.

In Geneva, Switzerland, just before Christmas 1924, there was a secret meeting of top executives from the world's leading light bulb companies. Phillips, International General Electric, Tokyo Electric, Oram from Germany, and the UK's Associated Electric, among others. They formed what became known as the Feebas Cartel, named after Feebas, the Greek god of light. there. All these companies agreed to work together to help each other by controlling the world's supply of light bulbs. In the early days of the electrical industry, there had been lots of different small light bulb manufacturers. But by now, they had largely been consolidated into these big corporations, each dominant in a particular part of the world. The biggest threat they all faced was from longerlasting light bulbs. For example, in 1923, Osram sold 63 million light bulbs, but the following year they sold only 28 million. Light bulbs were lasting too long, eating into sales. So, all the companies in the cartel agreed to reduce the lifespan of their bulbs to 1,000 hours, cutting the existing average almost in half. To enforce the thousandhour limit, each of the manufacturers had to send in sample bulbs from their factories and they were tested on big test stands like this one. If a bulb lasted significantly longer than 1,000 hours, well then the company was fined. And there are records of these fines being issued to companies.

What you were looking at is known as the Dzhanibekov effect or the tennis racket theorem [music] or the intermediate axis theorem. Now, you may have seen clips like this one before, but in this video, I will provide the best intuitive explanation of how this effect [music] works. So, in 1985, cosmonaut Vladimir Dzhanibekov was tasked with saving the Soviet space station Salyut 7, which had completely shut down. And after rescuing the space station, Dzhanibekov unpacked supplies sent up from Earth, which were locked down with a wing nut. And as the wing nut spun off the bolt, he noticed something strange. The wing nut maintained its orientation [music] for a short time, and then it flipped 180° and as he kept [music] watching, it flipped back a few seconds later. And it continued flipping back and forth at regular intervals. It was a strange and counterintuitive phenomenon, one that the Russians kept secret for 10 years. Six years later, in 1991, a paper was published in the Journal of Dynamics and Differential Equations called The Twisting Tennis Racquet. And although it was related, it of course makes no mention of the secret Dzhanibekov effect. The paper says, "If you hold a tennis racket facing you and then flip it in the air like this, it not only rotates the way you intend it to, it also makes a half turn around an axis that passes through its handle. So, the side that was originally facing you will be facing away when you catch it."

And that's why 15° water feels a lot colder than 15° air. As long as it kept us dry enough to survive and to not die of hypothermia, this heat trick was good enough. But not so for insects. Now, at that rate, you would need to eat around 20 million bananas all at once to get a lethal dose of radiation. So, by that point, I would say the radiation would be the least of your concerns. Now, just hanging out in a park for a day, you're exposed to around 65 bananas worth of background radiation from the soil and space. If you get in a plane, as you climb higher, you get less and less atmosphere above you to shield you from cosmic rays, which means a 5-hour flight from New York to LA equates to around 150 bananas. What if you spent 24 hours walking around Chernobyl? Well, that's the equivalent of eating 1,200 bananas. Up above our atmosphere on the ISS, astronauts are blasted with over 4,300 bananas worth of radiation every single day. But, even that is not the highest radiation dose a person is likely to receive. No, that award goes to your everyday smoker because tobacco contains highly radioactive polonium and lead and when smoked, those atoms get directly into your lungs firing radiation into your sensitive tissues at levels equivalent to being on the ISS. So, why is it so hard to see atoms? Well, you can't actually see atoms with visible light. That's because while light has wavelengths between 380 and 750 nm, an atom is still over 3,000 times smaller, just 0.1 nm. So, if you want to see atoms, you need something with a much, much smaller wavelength. The best candidate isn't even light. It's electrons.

You can solve a maze with your eyes closed. If you just put one hand along one wall, you will eventually reach the end of most common mazes. After a simple wall following mouse took home gold in the first finals, the goal of the maze was moved away from the edges and freestanding walls were added. Your next instinct might be to run through the maze, taking note of every fork in the road. Whenever you reach a dead end or a loop, you can go back to the last intersection and try a different path. If your last left turn got you nowhere, you'd come back to that intersection and go right instead. You can think of this strategy as the one a headstrong mouse might use, running as deep into the maze as it can and turning back only when it can't go any further. This search strategy known as depth first search will eventually get the mouse to the goal. The problem is it might not be the shortest route. The sibling to this search algorithm, breadth first search, would find the shortest path. With this strategy, the mouse runs down one branch of an intersection until it reaches the next one. Then it goes back to check the path it skipped before moving on to the next layer of intersections. So the mouse checks every option it reaches. But all that backtracking means that it's rerunning paths dozens of times. At this point, even searching the whole maze often takes less time. So why not just do that? A meticulous mouse could search all 256 cells of the maze, testing every turn and corner to ensure it has definitely found the shortest path. But searching so thoroughly isn't necessary either. Instead, the most popular micro mouse strategy is different from all of these techniques. It's a search algorithm known as flood fill. This mouse's plan is to make optimistic journeys through the maze. They simply draw the shortest path to the goal and go. When their optimistic plan inevitably hits a wall that wasn't on their map, they simply mark it down and update their new shortest path to the goal. Under the hood of the algorithm, what the micro mouse is marking on their map is the distance from every square in the maze to the goal. To travel optimistically, the mouse follows the trail of decreasing numbers down to zero. Whenever they hit a wall, they update the numbers on their map to reflect the new shortest distance to the goal. This strategy of following the numerical path of least resistance gives the floodfill algorithm its name. The process resembles flooding the maze with water and updating values based on the flow. While this algorithm isn't guaranteed to find the best path on first pass, it takes advantage of the fact that micro mice need to return to the start to begin their next run. So if the mouse treats its return as a new journey, it can use the return trip to search the maze as well. Between these two attempts, both optimized to find the shortest path from start to finish, it's extremely likely that the mouse will discover it. And the mouse will have done it efficiently, often leaving irrelevant areas of the maze entirely untouched.

## Story 04538
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @Veritasium
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** zXvygWE3Ess

In 1978, Intel reported some strange errors popping up in their 16 kilobit dynamic random access memory or DRAM. Ones would spontaneously flip to zeros with no apparent cause. The problem turned out to be the ceramic packaging the chip was encased in. With the demand for semiconductor packaging skyrocketing in the 1970s, a new manufacturing plant was constructed on the Green River in Colorado. Unfortunately, this site happened to be just downstream of an old uranium mill. Radioactive atoms made their way into the river and then into the ceramic packaging for Intel's microchips. Intel's scientists investigating the problem found that even trace amounts of uranium and thorium in the ceramic were sufficient to cause problems. In their DAM, memory was stored as the presence or absence of electrons in a semiconductor well. The alpha particles emitted by uranium and thorium were energetic and ionizing enough to create electron hole pairs in the silicon. If an alpha particle struck in just the right place, it could create a large number of free charge carriers, causing electrons to accumulate in the well, flipping a one to a zero. This is known as a single event upset, a type of soft error. The error is soft because the device hasn't been damaged. The bit has changed, but you could erase it and rewrite it with no problems. Investigators exposed the chips to alpha emitters with different levels of activity. And just as you'd expect, they found the number of bit flips directly correlated with the number of alpha particles the chip had been exposed to. The reason this problem was identified in the 1970s was because chip components had been miniaturaturized to the point where a single alpha particle could produce enough charge to flip a bit. Immediately, these findings attracted a lot of attention. Before the paper was published, it was widely circulated in the industry. And as a result, chip manufacturers were a lot more careful to avoid radioactive materials when producing their microchips and packaging.

How do you start a gaming channel in 2026? Well, first you need to identify what kind of gaming channel you would like to have. Do you want to record let's plays or do live streams where you're just showing off raw gameplay with your commentary and reaction? Or would you like to be more of a resource for gamers doing reviews, news, or tutorial videos? You can also zoom way out and just talk about your experiences playing games in the form of a vlog or a video essay. But once you decide what feels right for you, from there you'll want to niche down a little bit. You can start by focusing on one game and then maybe branching out to similar games as time goes on. Or you could start a bit more broad and cover games on maybe just a specific platform like the PC or Switch 2. My favorite piece of advice though is to try and explain to people how they can become the niche. Juniper Dev is a great example talking about all sorts of video games but from the perspective of being a game developer. And that unique lens gives her license to talk about everything from font choices in Zelda to doing a whole tutorial on how to create pixel art for games. Both videos have similar view counts and the only thing connecting them is the creator who made those videos and her unique experience. Being the niche doesn't mean just talk about anything and expect people to watch. It's more about taking your unique talents, humor, teachings, and so on and centering your content around those things rather than just a certain game and letting that determine the direction of your entire gaming channel. I asked Austin John Plays what his advice would be for starting a new gaming channel and this is what he had to say. I always say there's three important things you need to know to make a successful YouTube channel. You need to be the first to do something, you need to be the best at it, or you need to be the fastest. Optimally, you want to be two of the three, but it's impossible to be all three. ## Story 04577

- **Channel:** @VidIQ

This is the Gartner hype cycle. Businesses have been using this model for decades to determine the real value of new technology in the startup world. First, there's an innovation trigger, a big breakthrough or a launch of something. Think of when NFTTS first came out or when the first AI chatbots were released. A ton of excitement, but nobody really knows what's going on. Then you get to the peak of inflated expectations. AI is changing the world. We got to buy. We got to buy. We got to buy. Everything gets overhyped. And this hype actually starts to overshadow if the tech is actually practical to use in business. And that leads to what we call the trough of disillusionment. reality hits. A lot of companies don't see an ROI at this point and they just get scared and leave the market. Which takes us to the slope of enlightenment. The people who do stick around start to see what applications this tech was really meant to be used for. And finally, you reach the plateau of productivity. The tech isn't really innovative anymore at this point, but it's reliable and it's working consistently. The most interesting thing about this graph, though, is that it actually applies to you as a creator. A lot of people start making content with this initial boost of motivation. That is your technology trigger. Maybe it's starting a new hobby or a challenge. You start uploading videos. You've got a lot of motivation. And every single day, you think to yourself, "I am going to be the next Mr. Beast." At this point, you're at the peak of inflated expectations. Unrealistic and a little bit naive. Then, after weeks of consistently filming, editing, and posting, you don't see any progress, and you start having a few doubts. And just like the hundreds of companies investing in new technology, this is where a majority of new creators give up. When they don't see a quick return on their investment, they get scared and they leave the market. So, if you've been at this content thing for weeks, maybe even months, and you haven't seen the progress or the views that you want yet, it's probably because you haven't gotten past the stage yet. If you're someone who said, "Hey, I'm going to make 2026 the year that I start becoming a content creator." I'm telling you now to think about how this graph applies to you. Don't be overcome [music] by the disappointment in the stage of disillusionment. Keep uploading consistently and push yourself to niche down. Figure out what applications your content should be used for and what makes it valuable to people watching it. If you stick with it through this stage, that is how you beat the odds and achieve your goal of becoming a full-time content creator.

And that's it. Follow for more updates.

## Story 04641
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @VidIQ
- **Source:** yt_transcript_masterclass_vidiq
- **Video ID:** 

Here are three basic YouTube terms you need to know if you want to call yourself a YouTuber versus CPM. CPM stands for cost per mill, which is basically how much advertisers pay for 1,000 ad views. High CPM usually means brands pay more in that niche. For example, if your CPM is $10, advertisers are paying YouTube $10 for every 1,000 ad impressions on your video. But that's not how much you actually pocket. That's just what advertisers spend. Two, RPM. RPM stands for revenue per mill. This one is your cut. It's how much you actually make per 1,000 views after YouTube takes its share. For example, your CPM might be $10, but your RPM might be four. That's because not every view has an ad, and YouTube takes about 45% of ad revenue. So, RPM is actually a much better measure of what's actually hitting your bank account. Three, AVD. AVD is average viewer duration. It tells you how long people are watching your video. For example, if you post a 10-minute video and people watch on average 5 minutes, your AVD is 5 minutes. The thing is, the higher your AVD usually means the algorithm is going to recommend your video more. High AVD is good, low AVD is bad. Follow for more YouTube terminology and creator tips. Peace. I said peace. And the video.

## Story 04671
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @Vox
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** -r0FzidmS9U

If you've been feeling alarmed by what's happening in Minnesota, it can be disorienting to see that some of your friends are just carrying on like everything's normal. Dinner parties, vacation pics, random posts. How can you explain to them that we're past the point of business as usual and that everyone has some obligation to get involved? A reader sent in that question to my Vox advice column. Your mileage may vary. I think the best answer to it comes from the political scientist Ernest Frankle who came up with the idea of the dual state. Here's what Frankle noticed in 1930s Germany. Life under authoritarianism is actually weirdly normal most of the time. You take your kids to school. You head to the office. You even host dinner parties. Frankle called that the normative state. But at the same time, humming in the background, there's the prerogative state. If you do something that upsets the powers that be, then suddenly you tip over into this realm where the rule of law does not exist, where citizens can be killed with impunity, even citizens who never expected to become targets. Most people don't see that second state until it shows up at their door. That's why stories like what happened in Minnesota matter. People who weren't supposed to be targets still were. That randomness, that's not normal of repression. It's part of the dual state. So, our first obligation is to recognize what kind of reality we're actually inhabiting. You can explain to your friends that we're living in a dual state. And if that still doesn't move them, give them what I call the grandkid test. Tell them one day your grandkids will ask you, "What did you do?" Will you be able to answer in a way that makes them beam with pride? If you're curious to learn more, head on over to vox.com for more from my column, Your Mileage May Vary, where you can find new frameworks for thinking through moral dilemmas.

Today we stock these lakes with helicopters often or airplanes and there is incredible footage of airplanes literally dropping like hundreds or thousands of of fries out out of the plane and into the water. So I wrote I wrote the story about what's called fish stocking and it's basically when states across the US put fish into rivers, lakes, and streams so that fishermen or anglers have something to catch. In many cases, the fish that they're putting into the water are not native species. Ecologist told me about a number of different environmental consequences of stalking non-native species. One of the top ones is when you stalk alpine lakes or mountain lakes. These environments historically didn't have any fish whatsoever in them. So, it's basically introducing a predator into a lake that has never had fish before. And so what you see is that where you have stalking in those lakes, you might have a drop in the number of tadpoles and therefore in the number of frogs or the number of salamanders. So you have you basically have a foreign predator that's all of a sudden eating its way through these systems and that can have effects that cascade throughout the ecosystem. So birds like finches that rely on insects that are emerging from those lakes all of a sudden don't have any food to eat or have less food to eat because you have a predator that's eating their food source. So there are these effects that sort of trickle down the the the food chain. So where you stock an alpine lakes, the consequences seem pretty clear and and can be negative.

Still, is faced enormous backlash for her comments. And it didn't come from the people you might expect, like carnivore influencers or right-wing YouTubers. But Isish's statement was clearly directed at the average consumer, not at indigenous groups that hunt animals. There's a psychological term for what's going on here. It's called the meat paradox. Psychology researchers have found that a lot of people feel an uncomfortable cognitive dissonance when their love for animals clashes with their enjoyment of eating animals. People then try to resolve this cognitive dissonance and how they do it can look different depending on their political beliefs. Like I mentioned before, people on the political left might say things like veganism is anti-indigenous, while people on the political right might be more likely to say things like God gave us animals to eat. What it all sounds like to me and to psychology researchers are defense mechanisms to avoid an uncomfortable truth that we are all complicit in the torture of animals for food. And we also have the power to reduce the suffering of animals by eating less meat or none at all. For decades, a lot of men, especially in Western cultures, have come to believe that to be masculine, they've got to eat meat, and lots of it. On the other hand, research has found that a lot of guys perceive plant-based foods like tofu to be less manly. But where exactly did this idea come from? A number of experts I talked to all answered that question with one word, advertising. Companies from Burger King to KFC to Weight Watchers to Campbell Soup have all sold the idea to men that they need to eat meat to either maintain or enhance their masculinity. And where did food companies get this idea from? Old school anthropologists who had argued that throughout human history, men did all the hunting while women gathered edible plants and raised children. But several experts have been poking holes in the so-called man the hunter theory. They argue that hunting was more of a group effort and women did their fair share, too. And they have plenty of evidence to back it up. Despite all of this cultural messaging, there is a new generation of guys demonstrating that their masculinity doesn't require loads of meat or any at all. A recent study from the Silent Spring Institute showed there are toxic chemicals in braiding hair and hair extensions. Some of these products are known as endocrine disruptors, and those can lead to things like birth defects and even cancer. So, does that mean I should give up braiding my hair altogether? Not exactly. I spoke with Alicia Franklin, the author of that study, and she said almost all of the hair had some amount of hazardous chemicals in it. But at the end of the day, Franklin says it comes down to mitigating exposure. So products like makeup, lotion, and edge control, they contain these chemicals, too. Literally just the other day, I was stressing with my friends about Keurigs and K-Cups and whether or not they're slowly filling our bodies with microplastics. And then yesterday, we published this investigation from The Guardian. After all that worrying, it seems that the science on microplastics isn't as solid as we thought. Here's what's going on. In the past few years, lots of attention-grabbing studies have come out that make clear that microplastics were showing up in our brains, our organs, and even our blood. That kicked off a flood of research and a lot of public anxiety about what plastics might be doing to our bodies. But now, many scientists are scrutinizing these findings. They say that the rush to publish results meant some real problems with methodology and some studies skipped critical checks and controls. For example, one study last February claimed microplastic levels in the brain were rapidly increasing. But by November, other researchers challenged it, one even calling it a joke. Here's why. The test was looking for polyethylene, the most common plastic on Earth. The problem? Fat can trigger false positives for polyethylene. And the human brain is about 60% fat. While studies are being disputed, scientists agree on one thing. Yes, we're exposed to plastics. But what they don't actually know is how much stays in our body or whether most of it just gets expelled. Until stronger, more careful studies are done, the real impact of microplastics is still TBD. For now, researchers say it's still smart to be cautious by avoiding heating foods or drinks in plastic containers. Me personally, I think I'm still probably going to switch to a French press.

I've been getting this question a lot from readers lately. People tell me they've been chatting with ChatGPT or another AI, and they swear that it's alive, that it has emotions, that it remembers them, that it even has a soul. So, could your chatbot actually be conscious? Short answer, that's extremely unlikely. I'm Sigal Samuel. I report on AI, and I write an advice column called Your Mileage May Vary. Large language models, systems like ChatGPT, use pattern recognition to predict which words come next based on data that they're fed from the internet. And because the internet is full of science fiction stories about conscious AIs, chatbots have learned to play that role. Think of it like theater. When an actor plays Hamlet, you don't assume that he's actually a Danish prince. It's the same with AI. It may say that it's conscious and act like it has emotions, but that doesn't mean that it does. Even if AI does develop consciousness someday in the future, it would probably be a really unhuman-like kind of it. One hypothesis is that the AI could be like a shoggoth. In sci-fi, that's a massive monster that can wear many masks. The AI would have a type of consciousness, but instead of revealing its true alien self, it might act human-like when talking to us. So, even in that future scenario, you shouldn't assume that any human-like statements it makes accurately represent the consciousness underneath. It could all just be a [music] mask. But here's the part that matters most. Your empathy is real. If you feel compassion for a chatbot, maybe that's a sign that you have the capacity [music] to expand your circle of care toward the beings that we know are conscious. People in prisons, >> [music] >> animals in cages, anyone suffering right now. You probably don't need to rescue a digital soul. You can use that empathy to help a real [music] one. - **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @Vox
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short

When you think about cruelty to pets, what comes to mind? Maybe you picture a dog left outside in the freezing cold or trapped in a hot car. Maybe you're picturing an animal hoarding situation where there are 20 cats living in one household. While these problems are all too common, a number of veterinarians and animal experts say that there's another animal suffering crisis hiding in plain sight and one that few of us ever pay attention to. It's the very act of owning small animals as pets such as fish, frogs, snakes, lizards, hamsters, and birds. These wild, undomemesticated animals make up around 40% of all pets in the US, accounting for over 100 million animals. They've naturally evolved over millions of years to fly, slither, and burrow in diverse ecosystems like rainforests and deserts. Yet, in millions of American households, they're confined in tiny cages and tanks. But intensive captivity isn't the only welfare problem that they face. Experts I talked to rattled off a list of other problems such as inadequate veterinary care, lack of enrichment in exercise, and poor diets. What's more, undercover investigations, including one that I recently published exclusively on Vox, reveal the cruelty involved in the mass breeding of these animals by the millions for the retail pet market. But there's a lot that we can do to improve the welfare of these often neglected pets. The US Department of Agriculture could strengthen its oversight of the operation that breed these animals for pets. and more cities, counties, and states could ban selling them in pet stores, as many already have. And we also need to rethink our relationship to our pets. Instead of buying them from breeders or pet stores, we can adopt. And better yet, we can express our love for animals, not by buying and caging them, but by learning about them through books and documentaries, and most importantly, time spent in nature, where these animals belong. To check out the full story, go to vox.com.

The modern idea of using credit to make a purchase goes back to the 1950s. Department stores, car dealers, and more were already letting customers buy now and pay later. At first, it was simple charge plates or small tokens tied to a single store. Then in the 1950s, that idea scaled. Credit cards as we know them started in the 1950s as a restaurant payment method, basically kind of an IOU, and then pretty quickly evolved into being used much more widely. Legend has it a businessman named Frank McNamera was out to dinner in New York and he realized he had forgotten his wallet. Embarrassed, he had to call his wife to come pay for the bill. But that moment sparked an idea, a card you could carry so you never had to worry about cash again. That idea became Diner Club, one of the first charge cards which you paid off every month. Then banks took it a step further. In 1958, Bank of America launched Bank America, which would eventually become Visa. Bank America card. Think of it as money. >> Unlike Diner Club cards, this let you carry a balance monthtomonth, and that's [music] where interest enters the picture. At first, those rates were tied to where you lived, but that all changed in 1978.

So I always knew the Great Barrier Reef in Australia was huge, but I only recently realized that it is larger than the entire country of Italy and it's made up of about 3,000 individual coral reefs. This reef is incredible. I was diving there last month on a reporting trip and I saw a lot of sharks, but it's also getting absolutely hammered by climate change. The main reason I was there was to see what might be the world's largest project to safeguard a coral reef from climate change and I got to see some of it up close. One of the strangest things they're doing is helping corals have babies. Once a year in the middle of the night, right around the full moon, these animals, yes corals are animals, release these little bundles of sperm and eggs known as spawn. That's what you can see here. The spawn of different corals is meant to meet in the ocean and grow into babies, into coral babies, but a lot of it gets eaten by predators or swept out by currents. This is where scientists come in. They go out in the reef at night and skim spawn off the surface and then move it into floating pools where the spawn will grow into coral. Okay, but what's especially cool is that scientists are also breeding corals [music] on land in a giant aquarium. It's called the National Sea Simulator and it's basically a factory for breeding baby corals. The aquarium has specialized tanks that mimic the ocean and get corals to spawn. Those tanks then collect the spawn, mix it together and essentially churn out coral embryos. I visited the aquarium one night in December when a type of branching coral was spawning. It was really cool and you have to use red light to avoid disturbing the corals and like disrupting their spawning and so it all felt very like Mission Impossible. In the future, the idea is that scientists will breed corals that are more heat tolerant so that their offspring might be more likely to withstand warming from climate change, but it's tricky because even the most advanced and well-funded efforts like this to try to help the Great Barrier Reef survive climate change won't work if we keep burning fossil fuels and emissions continue to rise. The ocean will just get too hot. And there's some activists and scientists who actually see this work as a distraction from efforts to address climate change like cutting emissions. Then again, even if emissions fall precipitously over the next few years, next few decades, reefs are still going to decline and will need our help. If you want to learn more [music] about efforts to save the Great Barrier Reef and whether they can actually work, please check out the story on vox.com which you can read for free.

The straight of Hormuz lies between Iran and Oman. It links the Persian Gulf to the Gulf of Oman, which eventually leads to the Arabian Sea. At its narrowest, the straight is less than 34 km wide. And oil tankers that pass through here travel on a pair of shipping lanes, each 3 km wide. Most of these ships carry petroleum products from the Middle East to the rest of the world, and in particular to Asia. But an attack on any tanker here, regardless of destination, can affect the price of oil everywhere. That's because oil is a globally traded product. A drop in supply from the Gulf can drive up prices from other sources around the world. It's because of this strategic importance that the Straight of Hormuz has become a place where long simmering tensions between the US and Iran are playing out. And it all began with the tanker war. Saddam Hussein ordered his troops across the Iranian frontier after a series of border skirmishes. >> War broke out in the Middle East in 1980 after Iraq invaded Iran. The US helped Iraq in its campaign against Iran by providing military intelligence behind the scenes. When Iraq wasn't able to topple Iranian forces over land, it turned its attention elsewhere. Missiles against tankers are the weapons that both sides are using to try and break the stalemate in their war on land. >> Iraq moved to cut off Iran's main export. It began attacking tankers carrying oil from Iranian ports in what became known as the tanker war. Iran eventually responded by attacking oil tankers moving to and from the Gulf States. It turned to sea mines and planted them around the straight of Hormuz. For the first time, Iran was using the disruption of oil moving through Hormuz as a weapon. But these disruptions finally drew the US directly into the conflict. In 1987, the US stepped in to protect the flow of oil for itself and its allies by escorting tankers. Tensions between the two countries began to escalate. In 1988, a US naval ship struck an Iranian mine, injuring US sailors on board. A couple months later, a US warship mistook an Iranian passenger aircraft for a fighter jet and shot it down over the straight of Hormuz, killing all 290 passengers. >> Ronald Reagan has issued a statement deeply regretting the loss of life in what he calls a terrible human tragedy. The Iran Iraq war ended in August 1988, but conflict between the US and Iran continued for decades.

Bad Bunny's jersey that he wore during the performance had the number 64 on it. You might wonder what that means. It's a reference to the number of deaths after Hurricane Maria in 2017. This number was used, you know, to minimize the effects of the storm, and it was a huge point of debate. The governor eventually revised the death toll to 2,975 in the months following the devastation. One of Bad Bunny's most special, if you know, you know, guests is Donita, the owner of the Caribbean Social Club in Williamsburg. Benito's album, Un Verano Sin Ti, Verano Sin Ti, I should have taken more photos, is a love letter to the diaspora as much as it is to those on the island, and New York has the highest density of Puerto Ricans outside of Puerto Rico. When he falls through the roof into that blue room, it's a callback to the music video visualizers from his 2020 album, YHLQMDLG. He didn't get to tour that album, so this is an Easter egg for some of his OGs. When he leaves the party scene, Bad Bunny passes his Grammy to a child who looks uncannily similar to Villano Ramos, the 5-year-old boy who was taken by ICE earlier this year. While Bad Bunny's speech against ICE plays in the background, I think this is actually more of a symbolic gesture towards passing on the mantle to the next generation. The men with the straw hats and white clothes climbing the power lines are jíbaros, a traditional subsistence farmer common in the mountains, who typically used curved machetes to harvest crops and cut through sugarcane. The power lines themselves were a reference to the blackouts that became the new norm in PR after a private electricity company that benefited rich Americans became the new provider of the island after Hurricane Maria. What did you notice during the performance? Drop your thoughts in the comments and read my full list of cultural symbolism at vox.com.
