Most of the time, the framers thought that the president would not have very many troops under his command. But obviously, that's not how things worked out. The United States is the most powerful military in the world and the Constitution says that Donald Trump, the president, is commanderin-chief of that military. That said, there are two other possible limits on the president's war making powers. One is the War Powers Resolution, which says that the president should not use military force unless he has congressional authorization unless the United States has been attacked. But this law has never been followed. In a 2011 memo arguing that President Obama could use military force in Libya without congressional authorization, the Justice Department noted that previous presidents have used military force in Libya 1986, in Panama 1989, in Somalia in 1992, Bosnia in 1995, Haiti in both 1994 and 2004, and what was then known as Yugoslavia in 1999. The War Powers Resolution typically requires Congress to authorize a military conflict that lasts more than 60 days. So, if Trump's invasion of Iran continues for more than 2 months, well, we'll find out if that 60-day limit has any real teeth. The Constitution also says that Congress and not the president has to declare war, but modern nations rarely do that. It was supposed to be a compromise, but [music] by 2025, Wimbledon had eliminated the human line judges entirely. The challenge system wasn't the destination. To understand why, we need to examine the differences in the business models between the AI and social media industries. Hi, I'm Eric Levitz. I'm a senior correspondent for Vox, and this is a Reporter Extra.

How much do you trust the people moving through the world around you? Do you lean on the side of skepticism? Or do you place a lot of confidence in the people you meet? If you don't trust people and call yourself a skeptic, you're in line with how most of the country feels. The share of Americans who generally place trust in others has fallen to 30% since the 1970s when nearly half of Americans trusted others. There are many factors fueling this rise in distrust. Some experts have suggested economic instability, increasing diversity in the US, technology. But the real culprit seems to be political polarization. Trust is a necessary part of relationships. And while a bit of healthy skepticism might prevent you from falling into a scam or unsafe situation, a life of cynicism closes you off from really meaningful relationships. So, how can we extend just a little more of it? First, you've got to open yourself up to potentially being hurt. Scary, I know, but that vulnerability can breed really strong connections. And secondly, even if you disagree with someone over politics, ideas, values, we can choose not to assume the worst. People come from different lived experiences, and that doesn't make them inherently less trustworthy. Learn more about what experts had to say about building trust at vox.com.

## Story 04772
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @Vox
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** RBiRuJAyZAo

Today's Earth Day, and for some, it might be a reminder to start composting, to ditch fast fashion, or to carpool more. However, a lot of people in the environmental movement have become skeptical of the idea that individuals like you and me really have the power to fix climate change, and instead, they believe that all the responsibility falls on governments and corporations. I actually think they're largely right, but there are a few individual actions that can make a big difference for the planet, and one of the most impactful ones might not be what you'd expect. It's not composting or carpooling, it's eating a more plant-based diet. Here's why. First, meat and dairy production are just really inefficient ways to produce food. For example, you have to feed a pig 11 calories of feed crops like corn and soy to produce just one calorie of meat. Environmental scientists say we could feed more people with less land and resources if we just grew more food directly for us instead of funneling so many of our crops into farmed animals. Second, growing all these feed crops and setting aside so much space for cattle and sheep to graze gobbles up about 1/3 of the planet's usable land. That makes meat and dairy production the leading cause of global deforestation, habitat loss, and in turn, it makes it a top threat to wildlife. In comparison, lower meat and vegetarian diets require a lot less land, as you can see here. And lastly, there's climate change. Globally, meat and dairy production together are one of the leading drivers of climate change, accounting for nearly 20% of global greenhouse gas emissions. So, we could make a huge dent in climate change if we all ate fewer burgers and chicken nuggets and drink less milk. And that's because plant-based protein sources like Impossible Burgers, beans, and tofu have much lower carbon footprints. If you want to try out a more plant-based diet this Earth Day, we've got you covered. Check out Meat Less, Vox's practical guide to eating less meat and more plant-based foods at the link in our bio.

I want to tell you something cool that I just learned about giraffes. For a new study, scientists spent several years studying hundreds of giraffes in Tanzania and analyzing their markings. And what they learned is that the size and shape of their spots seems to affect how well they survive in different climate conditions. When it's unusually cold out, for example, the study found that giraffes with larger spots, like those you can see here, had a higher chance of survival. And the opposite was true when it was really hot out. Giraffes with smaller spots had a higher chance of survival. Basically, spot size and shape really matters in terms of whether or not a giraffe is going to live on. It's not actually totally clear why this is the case. But one explanation is that spots are darker than the fur around them and so they absorb more sunlight. So, a giraffe with bigger spots absorbs more heat and is more likely to overheat, which is fine or even great when it's cold, but not when it's hot. And this probably isn't surprising, but climate change is making the landscape that giraffes live in much hotter. The good news is that spot pattern is a genetic trait like eye color and height in humans. And that means that giraffes with favorable spot patterns, so if it's going to get really hot out because of climate change, we're talking about smaller spots, they might be able to pass on that spot pattern to their offspring, which might give their offspring a better chance of surviving in a warmer world. And what I think is so cool about this is that it suggests that giraffe spots are changing over time in response to what's going on in their environment. The question I guess is just whether those changes are going to be able to keep up with the rapid pace of climate change.

If you're spending $8 on a matcha for it to look like this, you're getting scammed. Let me show you how it's actually done. Matcha is made by grinding the leaves of a tea plant into a fine powder. It's a delicate art that's a central part of chanoyu, a traditional Japanese tea ceremony. So, when it's made in big batches with low-quality matcha, it can turn out bitter and lumpy instead of earthy and smooth. And yeah, matcha [music] is everywhere right now. Lattes, desserts, even cocktails. That's partly because tourism to Japan has surged, introducing more people to authentic matcha. As a result, demand is up and prices are rising. All while Japanese harvests are getting hit by extreme heat, and there are fewer people farming matcha. [music] In my research, I found this brand of incredibly high-quality matcha, which costs $42 for 1 oz. And this other ceremonial grade one costing $168 for 100 g, or about 3.5 oz. [music] So, if you're purchasing matcha either for your own use or at a coffee shop, here's what needs to be considered for it to be the best possible quality. First, it's important to know there are two types of matcha preparation. Koicha and usucha. Koicha is a thicker style of matcha used in traditional tea ceremonies. It has a higher concentration of powder to water, >> [music] >> and the powder is whisked but not frothed like usucha, which is the thinner style of matcha with less powder and more water. This is the style that we typically see in matcha lattes. [music] Second, water matters. Too hot and the matcha turns bitter. A good rule of thumb is to keep it around 176° Fahrenheit. Third, don't overdo it on the powder. A little goes a long way, and it's important to follow suggestive ratios for each brand. [music] And finally, not all matcha is created equal. Ceremonial grade matcha is made from the first harvest in the springtime [music] and is the highest quality. It's best for drinking straight, but you don't need the most expensive matcha if you're going to make sweet lattes with milk and sugar. Some places may try to get away with using culinary grade, which is cheaper, lower quality, and often used for cooking. So, double-check that it's ceremonial grade before you drink it. And if you are going to treat yourself to a matcha, make sure you're getting what you paid for.

Have you read the buzziest book of the year so far? It is called Yesteryear. It's by Carol Clare Burke. It is about a tradwife who travels back in time to 1855. Incredible premise. Could not put it down. But, when I finished the book, I did feel a little bit cheated. So, a lot of Yesteryear is focused on this idea that tradwife influencers are not telling us the whole truth with what they show us on camera, which we kind of already know, right? So, Burke sort of takes that a step further by showing us a scenario where her tradwife is not only like not a great mom, but also someone who really never wanted to have kids. She hates her kids. She doesn't want to be alone with them. She hates her husband. She hates her followers. At a certain point, she's on pills because she hates her life so, so, so much. And at the end, and I don't want to spoil too much here, but I will say that we learn that the tradwife herself is in some ways responsible for finding herself in the past. And she did it because she has this need to see herself be punished for all of her lies and the way that she's presented herself to the world. To me, that feels like we're taking this tradwife character and saying, "Oh, she secretly agrees with me and with my feminist critique of her life, and that's why she hates herself." I think it's perfectly possible to be a tradwife and even to be unhappy with your life and not really agree with my or Carol Clare Burke's, for that matter, feminist critique of the content that you have made. I think it's really possible to hold on to your very clearly stated beliefs about your life, even if you don't actually like the way that your life is going. On a literary level, that's a conflict that can actually be really interesting because it challenges us to think about how other people experience their own unhappiness. So, when Yesteryear goes the other way and decides that the only satisfying ending is for this tradwife to not just be miserable, but also be defeated by her feminist critics and ultimately agree with them, to me, it really feels like a missed opportunity. And so, even though I enjoyed this book, I did feel let down at the ending. But, I want to hear what you think. Did you read Yesteryear? Did you like it? What do you want from a tradwife novel? Let me know and head over to vox.com/culture to read my full review.

Heads up. This is Andrew. He's responsible for releasing fish into this river, but there's a catch. These fish aren't from here. They're not native to this river, and until the late 1800s, they never would have been found here at all. So, why are they being released here by state officials? Well, it turns out there's a very real, but kind of counterintuitive reason for this. There aren't a lot of fish thriving in ecosystems like this one. So, in order for people to enjoy the sport of fishing, they need to put fish into the water. It's a process called fish stocking, where fish are bred and released into the wild, often for the specific purpose of being fished out. And it's done all over the country using trucks, hoses, and even planes. For our trout stocking program, the goal is for 100% of the fish to be caught. So, they're being bred at hatcheries to be caught. That's right. Which does raise a big question for me. Is it reasonable to raise fish for the sake of sport? And what's the effect on the ecosystem that these fish are dropped into? So, I came up here to Connecticut to see some fish stocking in action and find out more about why this is such a common practice.

When we talk about animal shelters, we tend to focus on the animals themselves. The stray cats, the abandoned dogs waiting to be adopted. But there's another group that's suffering, too. Animal shelter workers. People who work in your local animal control department or animal shelter are on the front lines of America's pet overpopulation crisis. And as a result, they bear an enormous amount of stress. Collectively, they euthanize some 600,000 animals each year. and they also respond to countless animal cruelty and neglect cases. They also put themselves in harm's way, having to deal with aggressive animals and hostile pet owners. On top of all of that, they are overworked and underpaid, which leads to high turnover rates. As one worker told me, animal control officers and animal shelter workers get trauma heaped on them daily, but their experiences don't get much attention. Research has found that performing euthanasia on cats and dogs and other stressors of these jobs predicts higher rates of anxiety, depression, and substance abuse. But this isn't inevitable, and fixing America's pet overpopulation crisis would help both animals and shelter workers. A few changes from the government and pet owners would go a long way. Perhaps the most important solution is increased government funding for lowcost spay and neuter programs and getting more people to use these services for their animals. A lot of cats and dogs who wind up in animal shelters were accidents caused by someone failing to spay or neuter their pet. Another solution is increasing funding for animal control departments and animal shelters. They are nearly always short on the space and staff needed to care for all of the extra animals in their communities, which results in higher euthanasia rates. Governments could also crack down on puppy mills and ban the sale of cats and dogs at pet stores as a way to encourage adoption from animal shelters. Finally, there's a lot that you can do to support the workers and animals at your local animal shelter. You can give a donation, sign up to volunteer, foster an animal, or if you're ready to have one of your own, you can adopt a dog or cat looking for their forever home.

By certain operational metrics, flight scheduled or passengers flown, American Airlines is not only the largest air carrier in the US, but the largest in the world. And yet, by most financial metrics, margin, revenue, profit, it is the least effective of the Delta-United American trioplete at actually making money. It's a curious dichotomy, but it's not explained by simple bad luck. Since 2020, at almost every major moment in which the airline's management could have made the right or the wrong decision, they made the wrong one. It started with COVID. In those earliest days of the pandemic, the big strategic call each airline had to make revolved around timeline. The aviation industry quickly understood that the demand for short haul travel would likely recover sooner than the demand for long haul travel due to a combination of government regulation and passenger preference. Therefore, it was clear the wide body and long haul planes predominantly used for intercontinental flights would not be necessary any time soon. While each airline kept a portion of these planes operational for the skeleton international schedules they continued to operate, they each had to decide what to do with the rest of them. United kept them all. Some sat in short-term storage at their maintenance hubs, others were flown to longer-term storage at desert airports like Roswell, New Mexico, and Victorville, California. Delta took a broadly similar approach with the exception of its triple-7 fleets. These were old, comparatively inefficient aircraft that might not have remained in the airline's fleet for too much longer regardless, so Delta decided to just retire all 18 of them. American, meanwhile, clearly had a much more pessimistic prediction of what demand recovery would look like, and therefore they retired all of their A330, 767, and 757 aircraft representing a massive 30% reduction in their long haul fleet. It wasn't long before they regretted this decision. Fast forward to summer 2022, and Americans and Europeans were vexed and ready to travel. Transatlantic travel was up to about 90% of normal, and airlines were able to charge some of the highest fares ever thanks to the pent-up nature of this demand. People were willing to spend a lot after two years largely devoid of long haul leisure travel. American airlines, however, was only able to operate 80% of its normal schedule. They didn't have enough planes. Boeing was experiencing yet another manufacturing issue. The process for making small adjustments on the join between 77-feet-slotge components was not working properly. The connection between the parts was lumpier than it was supposed to be, so the FAA halted deliveries until the manufacturer could correct the issue. The consequence for American Airlines was that they didn't get 1377 deliveries that they were expecting before the high demand summer 2022 season. They said they wanted to operate 89% of their normal schedule, which would have been almost perfect for the 90% demand recovery that came into reality, but due to their aircraft shortage, they cut historically successful destinations like Edinburgh and Shannon. They were first on the chopping block because they'd previously been operated by the 757. The only smaller narrow-body plane equipped for Transatlantic service, so it was better to devote their wide-body aircraft to higher demand destinations. American also cut their floundering Seattle to London route and made even more brutal cuts to the other continents that composed their long haul network. Los Angeles to Sydney and Dallas to Santiago were gone, while frequencies went down to São Paulo, Beijing and Shanghai, all in service of reallocating aircraft towards the Atlantic. In the years that followed, Transatlantic demand stayed exceptionally strong. In 2023, Americans spent 17% more on international travel than they did in 2019 than the next year it went up to 27%. Just at the moment when the aviation industry came roaring back to life, American ended up with a smaller fleet meaning they could capture less of that expansion. The money went to United and Delta, American, lost out. But when Boeing finally resumed 77 deliveries, AA seemed to realize that not only did they mess up the timing of retirements, but also the composition, their long haul fleet was now over-indexed on newer airplanes. Newer doesn't always mean better for airlines because newer also means more expensive. These almost always lease or finance aircraft, so for cost of your newer airplanes, spare monthly payments are higher. These higher payments can be worth it because newer generations tend to be more fuel efficient. For every hour in airline flies a newer aircraft, they effectively recoup some of the costs of the higher payments through the fuel that they don't burn. So in order to make the trade from an older to newer aircraft worth it, there was a certain number of hours one needs to fly. American was struggling to reach those hours. Man

It's Friday, October 24th, 2025, and this place is buzzing. A popular month to visit, what's become a popular place to visit, waves of tourists wander the shops and restaurants of Cocosai Street. Splitting south, many then amble into the covered, ever-vibrant Makishii public market. It's hot, reaching 90 degrees Fahrenheit or 32 degrees Celsius, so many visitors, most hailing from mainland Japan, have taken to the island's beautiful beaches or the many national and nature parks littering the island, exploring the comparatively cooler climate of the subtropical forest. And they've taken to other islands entirely, hopping on fairies to explore the natural beauty of the nearby neighbors. Depending on their interests, the tourists spread. The foodie rambles the city, the freediver heads for the coral-lined coast. But on an island of only 463 square miles or 1200 square kilometers, or an island three-fourth of the area of Hawaii's Oahu, there's only so far the tourists can disperse. With some 700,000 visitors arriving a month and a permanent population of 1.5 million, 50% larger than Oahu's, this place is always buzzing. Of course, Friday, October 24th, 2025 is a bit of a holiday in itself in the United States. Friday's in fall mean high school football, and this school is no different as the KBHS dragons are set to host the Humphrey High School Blackhawks for a game that will crown a regional champion. Now Americans playing American football with American parents and fans in the stands on a school campus that neighbors at Kadova, Popeyes, and Burger King on one side, and the suburbs on the other, is not novel. But zoom out. This place, it turns out, is also that place, the tiny tourist island with the beaches, and both places are in Japan. This is Okinawa, an island that takes only two hours to drive across from tip to tail, and an island that hosts two entirely different worlds at once. This is the core of the American Air Force's presence in the Pacific, and all the America that comes with it runs headlong into a proud, distinct culture and a densely populated people. With little wiggle room, divergent goals, an imbalance in power, a complicated history, and an uncertain future, for decades there's been a lot of this and this and this as worlds and ways of life keep colliding. How things got so hotly contested on this little island is best summarized by the home of Kuba Saki High School's rival, Kadina High School. With 717 kids enrolled, it's the largest American High School in Japan. Largest is a theme here. By personnel at around 20,000, Kadina is the largest American airbase in the Pacific. The 18th wing, which runs the base and is capable of everything from fighting, to resupplying and rescuing, has the largest assortment of planes and helicopters at around 80 pieces of equipment. The wing support group, which handles everything from accommodations and logistics to budgeting, and the recreation and mayoral duties of the base, is the largest in all the air force. The base itself, hemmed in by urban Okinawa on both sides, boasts a golf course, countless parks and sports fields, and a host of suburban housing developments that from above look like they could be from anywhere in middle America. How this base ended up as the largest is directly linked to the event that gave Okinawa such widespread name recognition. Often recognized as the bloodiest battle in the Pacific, the battle of Okinawa was also the longest. What would prove to be the last major battle before Japanese surrender, overtaking these islands so comparatively close to the Japanese mainland, took months. Making landfall in April of 1945, US troops immediately took the then Japanese Kadina air strip. With the foothold in the center of the island, US troops have based their assaults from the area, including the common-deer airbase. Thus, by the time Okinawa fell, then Japan soon surrendered, the US had already set up operations on the islands. As World War II gave way to American occupation of Japan, and as the battle lines of the Cold War began taking shape with an increasingly aggressive North Korea looking to unify its peninsula and a rising Chinese Communist Party, Okinawa seemed a logical position to maintain a US foothold from an American perspective. It made sense from a Japanese perspective too. Because of Article 9 of this document, the 1945 Constitution of Japan, the country could not make war nor maintain an army. Because of this treaty, for someone to provide defense, Japan would rely on the US, allowing the US to maintain a presence within Japan. And because of Article 3 in this treaty, Okinawa, should the US want to take it on, would no longer be administered by Japan, but would rather be run solely by the United States. By the terms of peace largely dictated to it from the rest of the world, Japan had no reason to push back on the US expanding its presence on Okinawa. It was no longer theirs, and the bases on the island was served

If one headline could summarize the mood surrounding the cruise industry in 2008, it's this. Go buy the trip and short the stock. The advice was declarative. A year into the Great Recession, it made sense. If ever there was going to be a screaming deal on a vacation, it'd be now, and if ever there was a time to bet against companies built around discretionary spending, it'd be now. If anything, the advice was a little late. Carnival cruise lines, the biggest in the industry, was already trading at $16, or roughly half of where it was a year prior, while rural Caribbean trading in the mid-30s in 2007 had fallen to a measly $7 to share. But both could and would drop lower as travel businesses reliant on 90-100% occupancy rates to keep the lights on just didn't engender much investor confidence in the century's biggest economic downturn. For their part, though, the cruise companies got creative. Addressing what industry publications euphemistically called a challenging sales environment, they slashed room rates, they offered $200 on board credits, and they included two-for-one airfare to get there. As sign of the Times, Norwegian cruise lines, the third of the industry's big three, offered full refunds to those who booked a trip but could prove they had lost their job before embarkation. Wobbling, the aggressive maneuvering kept occupancy rates high enough to keep the companies upright. But doubts remained. Extreme discounting and last-minute deals could create difficult precedent to the economy bounce back. Cruising and leisure travel could take longer than other industries to bounce back to. And then the big one. How would these companies, emboldened by their past two decades of success and growth, but now struggling to find customers of any sort, feel the massive boats about to be delivered and activated in the next couple years? The industry's big three saw the moment a bit differently. Certainly, recession wasn't something they expected, and each readily admitted that the global downturn hard rocked their respective boats. It was in the first line in Carnival's annual report. It was the theme of the first paragraph of Royal Cribians, and it was responsible for the reported losses at Norwegian. But while each recognized things were rocky for the moment, they didn't share the belief that the industry had run headlong into an existential problem or that they had outkicked their own coverage. Rather, they saw revolution on the horizon. Royal Cribian pointed to its brand new class of ships that they were betting their future on Carnival had its own promising new boats on order, and Norwegian had just revamped its fleet to reflect an overhaul in its cruising style. If they could get through the downturn, they figured that the future of cruising, which they themselves were introducing, was bright. They had reason to project confidence. Until this point, the past two decades for the maturing industry had been red-hot. Since 1990, total passengers had tripled, really hitting a stride in the 2000s. And as the Cruisling International Association's 2009 slide deck acknowledged, even when off-skills arose, the graph still went up and to the right. With this graph, with its upward trajectory and recognition of passenger-resilience showed, was that the industry in the last two decades had zeroed in on its principal advantages. For a majority American customer base, cruising was the cheapest, most hassle-free means of taking vacation. Compared to researching a place, finding a hotel, booking airfare, then ground transportation, figuring out dinner every night, one could just call a travel agent, put down a credit card for a single flat fee and go on a trip, even leave the country without having to do as much as renew a passport. A cruise, with package airfare, meals accounted for, destinations vetted, and excursions already configured, offered both the budget-focused and travel weary a way to get out to good weather, or to see a corner of the world in a more comfortable way. Customer set as much. As these 2008 figures showed, multiple stops were important, but so too was the value and especially the ease of it all, which surveyed as far more important to cruise vacationers than non-cruis vacations. The problem was, this strategy had a cap. Leaning into easy and convenient, cruises had established reputation. They were for the newlywed and nearly dead. They lacked sophistication. They weren't cool, and especially for kids, they were just out and out boring. But with their mid-recessionaire of fleet expansion, Royal Caribbean was confident that it may have just bucked the trend that it may have come across a concept to making cruising cool. Their answer was this boat. When it launched in 2009, Oasis of the Seas was the largest passenger ship ever, but what's more important than sheer size was what it fit inside. The ship was split into seven distinct zones that Royal Caribbean called neighborhoods. The youth zone programme

In the United States, across all the government agencies that make this federal system go, the National Park Service is the one Americans like the most. This much makes sense. They're not collecting taxes, they're not investigating or surveilling, they're wearing friendly uniforms and welcoming people into America's favorite landscapes and historical sites. But in the nation's capital city, in Washington, D.C., they're viewed differently. In the U.S., D.C. is a jurisdictional and administrative anomaly. It's not a territory, it's not a state, and it's not within a state. It's a city that comprises a district, a singular status, not directly mimicked anywhere else in the U.S. or even really the world. As such, the rules are different here. Without a state's presence in park management, it is the task of the National Park Service to operate D.C.'s green spaces. That's 90% of the city's total park acreage spread across 350 separate sites, all of which varying in size and significance from tiny pockets of greenery to nothing short of the national mall. Problem is, while well prepared to look after the Yellowstones and Yosemites of the nation, the National Park Service is out of its depth when it comes to operating your run-of-the-mill city park. Take Lincoln Park, the largest on Capitol Hill in one of the busiest. Since 1791, when Kier Laugh Fawn dropped designs for the city, this park was meant for public use, and it's used by the public today for walks, for picnics, for frisbee, for a place to take the dog out. But for years, there's been tension over that last use. Dogs. Dogs are allowed in the park, but on a leash. Hurricane Katrina was an entirely expected storm. It was certainly a bad category 5 hurricane, but it was nowhere near the worst. Plenty more powerful storms have hit the mainland US since 2005, yet none came even close to the degree of human tragedy that Katrina unleashed. The reason this one became perhaps the most remembered modern US hurricane, even two decades on, has little to do with the weather itself. Rather, it's because the truest disaster was the response. In the days immediately before and after the storm, a series of operational failures unfolded, and the consequence was dozens, hundreds, perhaps even more than a thousand, preventable deaths. The earliest stages went as they should have. The US's weather agencies tracked the storm from the earliest phases of its formation, and as it rapidly intensified and its severity became clear about 48 hours out from landfall, the Federal Emergency Management Agency, or FEMA, initiated level 1 disaster operations at its headquarters, as did its two regional offices whose territory lay in the storm's path. From here on, all three offices would be staffed and working 24-7, while all 15 of the emergency support functions at their disposal would now be activated. Importantly, as the agencies aligned with these emergency support functions allude to, while FEMA's often assumed to be the federal arms solely responsible for disaster aid and relief, it's better understood as the agency tasked with coordinating response and aid. For help with major infrastructure problems, for instance, it'll coordinate with the Army Corps of Engineers, for firefighting, the Department of Agriculture, which houses the US Forest Service. Rather than run the show itself, it's the role of FEMA to listen to what local and state-level emergency offices need, then coordinate with other federal offices and the private sector alike to procure the physical goods or expertise required in the disaster-stricken area. But while not its primary intention, FEMA does still have some resources of its own, and these had been mobilized by noon on Saturday still two days out from landfall. The Region 6 office mobilized one of the agency's five mobile emergency response support units or murs to Camp Boergaard, Louisiana. On our moda of 275 vehicles carrying massive power generators, heating cooling systems, various telecommunication systems, multiple thousand gallon tankers of fuel and water and water purification systems, murs units represent the brunt of FEMA's on-the-ground capabilities. With each murs unit is also a mobile emergency operation vehicle, a truck-controller configuration capable of serving as a mobile headquarters and communications aid, and in this unit, goes by the name of Red October. In addition to the murs unit, FEMA staged nearly 550,000 liters of water, 700,000 pounds of ice, and 330,000 meals ready to eat at the camp, along with other supplies positioned elsewhere around the region. The day before landfall, the map of supplies and personnel deployed at the federal level looked like this, with an emergency response team member being appointed federal coordinating officer here in Baton Rouge, where federal response would coordinate with the state of Louisiana's office of emergency preparedness. The storm seemed massive. Experts believe that if it lived up to its billing that the levees surrounding New Orleans were likely to fail, but the response appeared coordinated and ready. But as Katrina barrelled towards the coast, signs of the response's potential limitations began to flicker. On account of high winds, not all water and food sent to supply the superdome, the major designated shelter, made it to the location, further limiting already constrained supplies. And some disagreement between New Orleans' mayor Ray Negan and FEMA head Michael Brown had already bubbled up on how to use the superdome to begin with, as the latter argued that it shouldn't be a shelter of last resort since engineering reports showed the structure may not even hold in the event of a category 3 hurricane. A third party seemed to draw similar conclusion, as the American Red Cross deemed the stadium too dangerous and refused to stage their own staff there during the storm, announcing they'd provide resources to the shelter only after it had passed. Part of the hesitation stemmed from the concern that opening up the facility could encourage people to stay in the city rather than evacuate, and in the days and hours when these hurried conversations were going on, the primary focus was to get as many people out of the city as possible. New Orleans had a specific evacuation plan that called for the southernmost parishes to leave first. These, Lafouche, Plaquemaine, St. Bernard, and St. Charles, were notified to start evacuations on Saturday at 9 a.m. by their parish leaders. That afternoon, Sheriff's deputies were managing the intersections and traffic lights were turned off to ex-bite traffic out of town. By Su

On the morning of Thursday, August 28, 2025, Amtrak trained 2154 pulled out of DC's Union station at 6.50am headed north, like it does every weekday. And like it does almost every other day, it quickly got delayed. By just its first stop, the train was 13 minutes behind schedule. In some cars, the automated doors weren't automating, so employees had to manually articulate it at each stop. The delays began to compound. 35 minutes late leaving here, 44 here, and by the time it arrived in Boston, the train was nearly an hour behind. Now for service that's not on time about a quarter of the time, such delays weren't particularly notable on their own. But while the route wasn't new, the train set was. And it came with big promises. This glitching northbound service was the maiden commercial voyage of the next gen Acella, a much anticipated brand new train set boasting increased capacity, increased comfort, and increased top speeds over the aging Acella as they were poised to replace. A decade in the making, the rollout of these new trains was anything but subtle, as these so-called Acella Bracian was marked by music, fireworks, speeches articulating that for Amtrak. For the future of passenger rail in the US, this was a moment in history. The moment, however, felt slightly less revolutionary on the ground. Beyond the slightly embarrassing malfunctions on Route 2154, riders were left unimpressed by the stiff chairs. They were amused by the outdated USB-A offerings that hinted at how long delayed the project had been, and they were quick to note that the train wasn't reaching the much anticipated top speeds of 160 miles or 260 kilometers per hour for long sustained periods. The Luke Warmerception continued from first-person reviews published everywhere from YouTube and Reddit to The New York Times. There were only five of these new Acella's active out of the 28 promised. There were no new routes or rail lines and thus no time gains. The trains were roomier, the new tilting technology was more comfortable, the food and wifi was better, the trains were fine. But they didn't feel revolutionary. But for Amtrak, for Amtrak users, for Americans hoping for better intercity rail, that's actually okay. In fact, this slow imperfect rollout that's far short of a revolution, but also far from being a disaster, is the latest example in a series of successes, many of them the small, quiet or imperfect variety that has Amtrak boasting some of its best numbers ever and training in the right direction even in the face of what many feared as an existential threat to the service in the form of the second Trump administration. To establish what counts as a series of subtle wins for America's largest passenger rail purveyor, it's important to establish Amtrak's real mandates. That's a quasi-public corporation that's managed as a for-profit organization, but this lacks some important nuance. Take the idea of for-profit, for example. In its founding legislation, the 1970 Rail Passenger Service Act, there was very limited flexibility in the language as it stated that the National Passenger Rail Corporation shall be a for-profit corporation. Ever since, the idea has been closely associated with the service. At the time this was a bold stance, but perhaps when they felt viable, given the fact that the service would be inheriting all its rolling stock from the floundering private passenger rail sector and it'd be borrowing rail access from the freight companies. A decision that still haunts the corporation's meager on-time percentage. In service by 1971, Amtrak by the end of 1972 was reporting a revenue of $160 million and an operating expense of $310 million. It was costing nearly double the year's revenue to keep passenger rail alive. Now early Amtrak and its sponsor, Congress, understood that it would take some capital investment up front to get rolling, but quickly it also began to recognize that running Amtrak for-profit was probably beyond the pale. In 1978, a small edit within the Rail Passenger Service Act acknowledged as much. With the addition of four words, that the corporation shall be operated and managed as a for-profit corporation, Amtrak's mandate from Congress quietly became more flexible. It was a subtle concession that saving a deeply unprofitable and thus cratering private passenger rail system in the U.S. was not exactly going to be a money maker for the U.S. Now turning a profit was not a necessity or an expectation, but operating in a similarly ruthless bottom line oriented manner of a for-profit was, this difference matters, but this difference is also easy to overlook. A common talking point on Amtrak is that in its 50 plus years of existence, the servic

Described derisively, the National Football League acts as a structure through which young men can sell the entertainment value derived from the destruction of their brains. Detractors and fans would debate that statement endlessly, but to the league itself, what's true hardly matters. What matters is what people believe. One can hardly overstate just how colossal the NFL is. Whether by revenue per team, revenue per game or just revenue outright, it is by far the largest sports league in the world. It's annual revenue is similar to that of McDonald's. Just one team, the Dallas Cowboys, is about as big financially as planet fitness, the largest gym chain in the world. The only live broadcast to have ever been viewed by more Americans than a Super Bowl was the Apollo 11 moon landing, while each week, about one in every 16 Americans tunes into their Sunday Night Football broadcast, making it the most successful TV program to have ever existed. But this supremacy is facing a reckoning because more and more people are coming to believe that American football is bad. More and more people are being convinced by the scientific evidence that American football frequently causes permanent damage to the brain, leading to debilitating cognitive dysfunction later in life. The immediate risk to the league's financial interests is not that people are going to tune out on mass out of discomfort with the way their dollars incentivize neural destruction. Football has always been perceived as a dangerous sport. It's something different, but something that the NFL is taking seriously as one of the biggest long-term threats to America's most popular contact sport. But as a point of comparison, take, ice hockey, America's second most popular contact sport. Well, the national hockey league has admitted the danger of brain injuries in its sport and implemented changes to mitigate their impact, the league appears far less concerned than the NFL in the threat to its financials. But on the surface level, it looks like they should be. According to Harvard Research Into The Matter, the NFL has a per game per player concussion rate that's 3.8 times higher than that of the NHL. In a given game, a professional football player is far likely to get a concussion than a professional hockey player, but what that actually means in practice is that in a season, the hockey player is likely to get a concussion since the NHL schedule includes 4.8 times more games than the NFL. Other studies back this up. Ice hockey has a similar, if not slightly, higher rate of concussions than American football. The thing is though, a single concussion is rarely a long-term issue. Typically, although not always, it's a short-term problem involving short-term cognitive impairment. A high school athlete might struggle to retain knowledge for a test, they might become irritable more often with their parents, they might be highly sensitive to light and noise, but typically the symptoms subside within a couple of weeks. A subset of concussions do lead to long-term or permanent issues, and the likelihood of these goes up with each additional concussion one receives, but permanent, cognitive impairment, specifically from sports-induced concussions is rare, and it's even rare that these permanent impairments are significant. Concussion rates are a misleading indicator for the volume of cognitive disability a sport is likely to create. Ice hockey players frequently skate faster than 20 miles or 32 kilometers per hour, which leads to some very big hits, but compared to football, these hits happen at a different rate. Checks are only legal against a player in possession of a puck and can only be made from the front or to the side. Checking from behind is strictly prohibited, punished with some of the costliest penalties in the sport, is it can lead to one's head or spine absorbing the brunt of the impact into the boards. Typically, checking is also focused against the boards whose glass flexes and absorbs some of the impact since offense can more effectively evade defense in center ice. When put into practice, this means that hits are relatively infrequent. One study found that men's college hockey players experienced an average of 287 head impacts in a season. In football, though, head impacts occur between 1,000 and 1,500 times a season, and potentially even more with certain positions. To those that know the game, this makes sense. The action that most commonly ends a play is a player on defense tackling the ball carrier, which frequently involves a head impact. The primary way the offensive side stops that from happening is by physically blocking opposing players, once again, frequently involving a head impact. The data suggests that a given head impact in football might be less likely than one in hockey to cause a concussion, perhaps due to the higher speeds on the ice, but each one of those more frequent impacts in football adds to a hefty accumulated sum of head trauma

Newark Airport sucks. That's not just an opinion, that's an objective characterization backed by data. Each year, the FAA publishes an on-time ranking of 30 major American airports and each year, Newark Airport sits near the very bottom. In 2015, it was 3rd to last. In 2016, last. 2017, last again, then 2nd to last, 2nd to last again, then back to dead last once again in 2020. Since then, Newark's ranking has risen, reaching a high of 17th out of 30 in 2024, but this was done largely not through Newark improving, but rather other airports getting worse. Various publications have attempted to evaluate airport quality in more nuanced ways, looking beyond simple on time ratings and incorporating a wider spectrum of qualitative and quantitative metrics to create an index score ranking major airports. According to these, when you look at an airport as more than just a machine that's on time or not, Newark still sucks. According to the Wall Street Journal's 2023 large airport rankings, Newark is worst. According to AirHelps 2025, Index Score of airports, Newark is worst, at least in America. According to JD Powers 2024 North American Airport Satisfaction Study, Newark is worst once again. In the overwhelming complexity of the Air Transport world, with so many different things that can go wrong, it's rather remarkable that this specific airport manages to be so consistently, superlatively bad. It indicates that something is fundamentally structurally wrong with the design of Newark Airport. A glimpse deeper at the data starts an explanation. Newark is never great, but through each year there's an ebb and flow from bad to terrible with a clear pattern. October, for example, is consistently its smoothest month. Over the past four years, it's earned an average on time ranking of 15th out of the 30 major American airports. March is also alright at an average of 18th. But what the data really demonstrates is that there are two core months of chaos, June and July. In these months, an average of just 65% and 62% of flights arrive in the part from Newark on time, an absolutely catastrophic level of consistency. And this makes perfect sense. So the west of New York City are the Appalachian Mountains. They're not America's most formidable peaks, having spent hundreds of millions of years eroding into their lumpy dollar form, but they're significant enough to affect the region's weather patterns. The prevailing wind pattern in North America is west to east, and this force pushes warm, moist air from the Gulf of Mexico over the Appalachians. As this air collides with the topography, it's forced upward into higher altitudes. At higher altitudes, temperatures are cooler, and as that ascendant air cools down, it loses its capacity to hold as much moisture. So the moisture condenses together, and that's what forms clouds. As those clouds drift eastward, they interact with warm afternoon air and cool sea breeze from the Atlantic, and this sort of atmospheric instability is what triggers thunderstorms. Thunderstorms and airports are not compatible. Airports can at least partially operate in a lot of weather conditions, snow, wind, rain, fog, but thunderstorms bring wind with a dangerous mix of intensity and unpredictability. It changes direction or strength rapidly, which could catch a pilot off guard in those critical moments before touchdown. So when a thunderstorm is at or near an airport, inbound flights often have to hold, go around, or divert, while outbound flights are often left awaiting for the cell to blow through. But then there's the matter of ground operations. Lightning strikes are, of course, dangerous to humans, and while there is no single national standard, Newark, like most US airports, shuts down baggage handling, pushback, and any other operation that involves workers on the ramp when a lightning strike is detected within its vicinity. Even a 20-minute shutdown can have cascading effects for the rest of the day, both at Newark and in the rest of an airline's network. But Newark isn't Newark City's only airport. There's also La Guaradiya and JFK near Miles away. And while there on time ratings are not great, often well below the national average, they're certainly consistently better than Newark's. Geography can explain this, at least in part. JFK in particular benefits from its eastward position on Jamaica Bay right near the Atlantic Ocean. Storms track from west to east, and therefore tend to moderate an intensity by the time they reach the airport, and the cooler sea breeze also dulls their strength. But their differences in performance are also explained by what they look like on the ground. Comparing the airports to scale, Newark is small. JFK is busier than Newark, but even compared to its closest equivalent in passenger numbers, Houston and San Francisco, the airfield is dwarfed. Relative to its busyness, Newark just doesn't have a lot of space, and this leads to some very real operational bottlenecks. It

In 2014, an Iran-back rebel group of Houthis overthrew the government, and the Saudis led an opposition coalition since they viewed the conflict as a proxy fight with Iran. This year's large contribution to Yemen demonstrates their continued commitment to that fight. Like all oil-producing countries along the Persian Gulf, Oman's authoritarian structure allowed the ruling party to reap disproportionate financial benefits from fossil fuel sales and distribute profits less evenly in the form of subsidies to its residents. But as the future of oil becomes less stable and countries look to diversify revenue, one of the options is taxation. Oman became the first country in this area to institute an income tax this year, and though it's just 5% and applies only to those making more than $109,000 year, the country's neighbors are closely watching, wondering if authoritarianism can survive taxation. The United Arab Emirates is also looking to diversify, and a newer core part of that strategy centers around pouring money into AI. The UAE signed an agreement with the United States to build the world's largest AI data center outside of America, potentially giving it access to more than half million in video chips. One way or another, we use shoes to understand others, and so we in turn select shoes to help frame how people think of ourselves. Shoes and shoe selection tell stories, and Nike used to be the best storyteller. If you don't know the story of Nike's rise, it's laid out across the map of the company's campus. At the end of one-bowerman drive, named after the company co-founder, on the shore of Lake Nike, neighboring the Michael Jordan and John McElroy buildings, and across from their Ronaldo field, is Steve Prefontaine Hall, appropriately at the core of the campus. Few characters embody the Nike story like Steve Prefontaine or Pre, and that's by design. Before Nike was a giant, before it was even called Nike, it was little more than the young, ambitious Phil Knight, a lifetime runner himself, importing sleek Japanese running shoes. It was a disruptor, a plucky underdog, a fighter looking to carve out just a bit of market share in a nascent American jogging boom at the beginning of the 1970s. At about the same time that Nike started getting momentum, a scrappy runner from an old coastal logging town in Oregon was beginning to rise. Prefontaine ran distance events for the University of Oregon under Coach Bill Bowerman, who was also designing running shoes for Nike. He was pre-begin to establish himself as America's best distance runner, it was only natural that he joined the brand. But Pre's central to Nike's rise, not just because he was good at track, but because of how he went about being good at track. Prefontaine won a host of collegiate titles, he set American records, he went to Munich in 1972 to represent the US at the Olympics, he was an undeniable talent. He was also a character, he was a working class kid from a tough industrial town, he was small, scrappy, handsome, and brash, he was cocky, but he backed it up with a work ethic that still revered in American running culture. He appeared on the cover of sports illustrated at 19, his races were aired on national TV, his go pre-T-shirts became so popular they were met by a backlash of stop pre-shirts, he was an American star and he wore Nike. When signed to the company, pre-rept Nike shoes at Run Clubs and public meetups, he traveled to high school and college programs to demo them, he sent them to friends and rivals around the world, he pushed the product and the producer, a brash upstart of its own, embody the kid's spirit. Nike was doing it their own way too, with shoes that pushed the boundaries first with the Nike Cortez that added mid-soul cushioning for runners. They then took the technology another step forward with the Nike waffle trainer designed to be lightweight and grippy while staying comfortable and padded. Ultimately, a fatal car crash claimed pre before he was able to wear his ow

## Story 04838
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @Wendoverproductions
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_doc_short
- **Video ID:** XcbAhYQ49_U

Europe and North America are the two most culturally and economically interlinked continents in the world, yet an ocean stands between them. It co-loss a number of people travel between the two for work, for family, or for fun, making the North Atlantic the largest and most valuable long haul aviation market in the world. Logic would then follow that it's the most competitive long haul aviation market in the world, and while it might look that way, sitting at JFK Airport, watching dozens of different European airlines roll by, the logos painted on the side are merely a façade. On the surface, it appears that 33 different airlines fly 126 different routes between New York and Europe. These are not only the big players like B.A., Air France, and KLM flying to major destinations like London, Paris, and Amsterdam, but also plenty of smaller airlines like Air Serbia, High Sky, or Azores Airlines serving smaller markets like Belgrade, Bucharest, or Punta del Gata. This appears like a healthy competitive landscape, until you look closer. If you wanted to fly, say, to Brussels, you'd have a choice between a 6-10pm departure from JFK on Brussels Airlines, or a 6-30 from Newark on United. But what you wouldn't have is a choice on how much to pay because almost every single day, the airlines are charging the same price. But there's a good reason for this. Brussels Airlines and United are effectively acting as a single airline. It's the same thing for Munich. United operates a 515 from Newark, Lufthansa a 530 from JFK, then another Newark frequency at 830, all priced identically. This is a cartel. Not a drug cartel, but a business cartel. Brussels Airlines, United, Lufthansa, as well as Swiss and Austrian airlines are all colluding. Together, they decide how often to fly, where to fly, and crucially how much to charge. Individually, they don't care who flies what, because they pull Trans-Atlantic revenue, then distribute it according to the terms of their joint venture agreement. It doesn't matter to United whether a passenger takes them or Lufthansa to Frankfurt, they'll learn the same amount regardless. This all means that, from the perspective of competition economics, these five airlines are really acting as one. This is intriguing. If you refer to Article 101 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union, it reads, quote, The following shall be prohibited as incompatible with the internal market, all agreements that directly or indirectly fix purchase or selling prices. It's hardly a secret that these airlines are price fixing, they'll happily discuss it with their investors. This would leave any normal business in a world of legal trouble, but the United and Lufthansa-led cartel has this. In this document, the European Commission outlines their decision to exempt these airlines from antitrust law. This is a legally approved cartel. European and American regulators have allowed these airlines to collude against the consumer, and it's not the only such case. The next largest encompasses Delta, Virgin Atlantic, KLM, and Air France, representing about 26% of the Trans-Atlantic market from New York. Then the third major Trans-Atlantic joint venture includes American Airlines, Airlingus, British Airways, FNAIR, Iberia, and Level, operating a further 18% of flights. Together, this sums to 75% of all year bound flights from New York. 75% of all flights in the most lucrative long-haul market in the world are operated by just three entities. Regardless, it is still true that New York has perhaps the most robust European competition of any U.S. airport, but that means that elsewhere, the problem is even worse. In the smaller market of DC, for example, these three joint ventures encompass 85% of all Trans-Atlantic flights, severely cipheling competition, and that stat will go up to 89% soon if SAS and ETA Airways are granted approval to join the Sky team and Star Alliance joint ventures as they intend. Now, the majority of the legal proceedings allowing for such collusion have taken place just a subway right away from Delus Airport at the U.S. Department of Transportation. American law does allow the DOT to grant antitrust immunity to airlines, but there's one key stipulation. They must mean that the public benefits enabled by collusion outweigh the negative consequences of reduced competition. Therefore, airlines submit long winding arguments to the DOT explaining why, in their opinion, the public would benefit so greatly from their collusion. One of the most recent cases took place between 2012 and 2019 as Virgin Atlantic sought inclusion into Delta's existing Trans-Atlantic joint venture to start they pursued antitrust immunity on U.S. UK routes. Their legal argument was all in support of this one line, quote, the joint venture will be a more effective competitor on U.S. UK routes. They explain that the crux of the public benefit would be, quote, the creation of a meaningful competitive alternative to the allianc

I play piano on the internet a lot and honestly I haven't been practicing enough dumb to realize I don't know what my hands did. Usually I'll hit record and mess up and then do a few more takes until it's good enough for the edit. But I can't do that on tour. You know, there's no editor up there to save me. If I mess up live, I just kind of suck on stage and nobody wants that. Which means I actually have to practice. For me, that means practicing modes. Pandas know they're the key to improvisation, but running them in every key is a total grind. I need a way to make that feel a little less lonely and a lot more focused. That's why I've been using Tonic. It's a music app that allows people to drop in while I practice. It gives me that feeling of a live performance, but in a safe space to where it doesn't feel scary or judgmental like being on stage. And when chat cheers me on during my practice, I actually want to keep going. Plus, Tonic tracks what I've worked on and how much I practice. So, when I ask myself in the mirror, how much of you actually practice this week? I finally have an answer. Yes. And yeah, seeing that progress feels pretty good, dude. If you want a way to keep your practice organized, focused, and actually fun, check out Tonic. It's free and you can actually follow me there. Link in description.

I could feel the gate inching nearer and nearer. So in the heat of the moment, I very wisely decided to do the exact same thing that didn't work the first time. I hit the reverse button, hit the gas pedal, and once again my car moves forward. At this point, the only thing I've managed to do is get more in the path of the gate. And before I could try my foolproof strategy a third time, it crashed into the side of my car and the alarm went off and it made a big ol' scratch that'll probably cost a couple hundred dollars to get buffed out and repainted. I don't know the exact amount yet because I'm too embarrassed to tell anyone what happened. Anyway, I've had about a month now to process this and I'm of two minds. On one hand, it is kind of hilarious that I waited nine years to buy a new car and I couldn't even get through one full week without fucking it up. I would love to see the security footage of me stopping in front of a closing gate, quickly accelerating, stopping, accelerating again, stopping again before getting out and yelling, God damn it! It probably looked really funny. But then on the other hand, I am kind of like, well, what the fuck happened? Because like, I know that I hit the reverse button two separate times and both times my car just decided not to do it. I tried recreating this exact scenario in a parking lot to pinpoint exactly where I went wrong. And my best guess is just that I hit the accelerator and the reverse button at the same time, which triggered the cars computer to be like, hey, you can't do that. You're gonna hurt the transmission! Let's hurt the side of the car instead. And like, it's a good thing that there's a mechanism in place to prevent the car from changing gears when you're moving too quickly. If I hit the reverse button while driving 70 miles per hour down the highway, I actually would want my car to ignore that. I'm pretty sure there was a similar mechanism in my old car. The difference is though that if this exact scenario happened while I was driving that one, there would have been physical feedback to tell me it was still in drive, and maybe I would have been less likely to floor it twice. In order to get the same confirmation from a button, you have to press it, look down at the dashboard, find the part that indicates what gear the car is

## Story 05209
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @illymation
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_animated_story
- **Video ID:** evjYGGCi2e8

The brain recycles unused serotonin, that's the happy brain chemical, for other stuff the body needs, like healing wounds, digesting food, helping you sleep, all kinds of stuff. But the brain always makes sure to leave just enough extra little serotonins floating around for later use. You know, in case you see a puppy, you have to say, "Oh my god, he's so cute. What's his name?" And the serotonin goes flying. Then some get recycled and some stay for the next puppy or whatever makes you happy. and rinse and repeat. But in a depressed brain like mine, the leftover floating serotonins may not be enough. So if I saw the same puppy, I might be like, "Oh, wo." All right, dude. Come on. It's a puppy. And this is where an SSRI can help. An SSRI stops the brain from repackaging as much serotonin, thus allowing more of it to be available, which in turn gives you more serotonin to access later to feel happier. Here.

## Story 05212
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @illymation
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_animated_story
- **Video ID:** LHLyTZQYYFs

My mom grew to have a better connection with Maya than anyone else in the family, but she didn't want to go alone, so I went with her. And even though Maya was a hissy little thing for all the years I'd known her, I found myself getting emotional on the car ride to the vet. I don't know what came over me, but once we were there, sitting on the floor talking with the vet, I wanted to hold Maya. And for the first time ever, she didn't growl or hiss. She let me pick her up and lay her down in my lap. I pet her. I kissed her head. And soon, she fell asleep. And when she was gone, I found myself crying so hard. I thought my mom would be like this, but no, it was me. And I still get choked up sometimes when I think about Maya. I guess you don't need to be super close or have the perfect connection to miss a pet or to love them.

The button can summarize an entire video. So, you know, a lot of time you'll watch a video and they'll say, "We're going to review the new Kia EV6, and we're going to tell you the six reasons we think this car is awesome and the one reason you might not want to buy it." The reason this button sucks for creators is you can click it as soon as the video starts, get the answer to the question you're looking for, and peace out. Before I got recognized all the time as an animator on YouTube, uh I was performing a lot at state fairs doing like a a comedy act and there was this really nice kid that messaged me saying, "I'm so excited for your show. I can't wait to see it." And I was so thankful to have him there. Um and I was like, "It's going to be great. Uh I'll use you in the act. I have some, you know, gifts for you." He came. He got there an hour early, sat in the chair. Showtime came. Him and his dad were the only people in the audience. And I felt so bad. I started doing the show, tried to get some more people in. Um, and they just weren't there. It was like a Tuesday. There was nobody there. So, I ended up ending the show and I told the sound tech, "I'll be back in 20 minutes. I'm going to take this kid and his dad out to ice cream." And, uh, we walked down the fairgrounds and I got him ice cream and we hung out for like 20 minutes. Anyways, that was a great memory for me.

## Story 05382
- **Weight:** 4
- **Channel:** @itsalexclark
- **Source:** youtube_transcript_animated_story
- **Video ID:** _ZVxPtrlP7A

I spent 800 hours animating episode 6 of The College Girl. It's a 15-minute movie with advice on how I survived my most toxic semester, explained with zombies. It cost at least 36,000 to make because senior animators in the US typically make between $40 and $70 an hour. 800 hours time $40 equals $36,000. Now, I've made no money this year because I've been so focused on these videos. So, I think it's fair to say it cost me that much to make. I'm hopeful the series will take off one day. But even if it doesn't, I'm happy I made it. Not just because of the overwhelming positivity I see in the comments, but because one day my kids will watch it. And the series is about how I met their mom. So, it's something they'll be able to watch forever. And for me, that's worth way more than $42,000.

There's been an influx of people going back to old tech as a way to step out of the overwhelming and predatory modern innovation era, and I am also on that boat. A while back, I started playing on my old 3DS, and something about using a classic handheld rewires my brain in the best way. Being all bundled up with your DS, no second monitor content, maybe some snacks. It feels like my mind gets to heal from the constant rot, cleansed from the addicting flashing colors and stimuli of current media.
